AI-Assistance-Quality-Criteria.title: AI Assistance Quality Criteria
AI-Assistance-Quality-Criteria.content:
AI Assistance Quality Criteria
This note compiles all AI-assistance capabilities for quality evaluation in a knowledge base.
Overview
AI can assist with continuous knowledge base maintenance through automated evaluation, detection, and suggestion systems.

1. Link Quality Evaluation
Capability: AI can automatically evaluate link quality
Test Criteria:

 Can AI identify weak links (decorative, not meaningful)?
 Does each link pass the “why follow?” test?
 Is there link inflation (>5 outgoing links per note)?

Implementation:

AI reads both source and target notes
Evaluates if connection adds conceptual value
Checks for reciprocal backlinks where meaningful
Flags notes with excessive weak links

See: AI-Assisted Link Quality Evaluation

2. Orphan Detection
Capability: AI can automatically detect orphaned notes
Test Criteria:

 Run graph analysis to find notes with 0 incoming links
 Verify all notes are reachable from _root.md in ≤3 hops
 Identify notes with < 2 total connections

Implementation:

Query for nodes with degree = 0
Trace reachability from root
Flag unreachable clusters

See: AI-Assisted Orphan Detection

3. Connection Suggestion
Capability: AI can suggest meaningful connections between notes
Test Criteria:

 Can AI find semantically related notes that aren’t linked?
 Do suggested connections pass the “why follow?” test?
 Are suggestions diverse (not just nearest neighbors)?

Implementation:

Embedding similarity search
Keyword overlap analysis
Temporal clustering (notes created close together)

See: AI-Assisted Connection Suggestion

4. Note Splitting
Capability: AI can identify and split oversized notes
Test Criteria:

 Can AI detect notes > 300 words?
 Can AI identify multiple ideas within one note?
 Can AI create proper atomic notes after split?

Implementation:

Word count thresholds
Topic segmentation
Link rewiring after split

See: AI-Assisted Note Splitting

5. Graph Health Monitoring
Capability: AI can continuously monitor overall graph health
Metrics to Track:






























MetricHealthy RangeAI CheckLink density2-5 links/note✓Orphan count0✓Reachability100% from root✓Hub distributionNo single hub >30%✓

Human-in-the-Loop Principle
AI evaluates, recommends, and alerts—but human confirms for:

Deleting orphans (might lose subtle value)
Merging notes (might lose granularity)
Splitting notes (might misjudge atomicity)

Auto-action risks losing context that AI can’t fully appreciate.

AI-Assisted-Connection-Suggestion.title: AI-Assisted Connection Suggestion
AI-Assisted-Connection-Suggestion.content:
AI-Assisted Connection Suggestion
AI can suggest meaningful connections between notes that humans might miss.
Capability
AI systems can analyze notes and suggest:

Related concepts based on semantic similarity
Contrast points with existing notes
Analogy bridges between domains
Category groupings for MOCs

Quality Criteria
Test: Can AI suggest connections humans would value?

Check: Are suggested connections surprising but valid?
Check: Can you explain why the connection makes sense?
Check: Do suggestions increase graph density without inflation?

How It Works

Semantic analysis — AI reads notes, identifies key concepts
Similarity scoring — AI compares concepts across notes
Suggestion generation — AI proposes connections above threshold
Human validation — Human confirms or rejects suggestions

Best Practices

AI suggests, human confirms — don’t auto-link
Prioritize cross-domain connections (highest value)
Review suggestions regularly (weekly)

AI-Assisted-Knowledge-Management-Seed.title: AI-Assisted Knowledge Management Seed
AI-Assisted-Knowledge-Management-Seed.content:
AI-Assisted Knowledge Management Seed

Drop this file into any vault to bootstrap a healthy, self-improving knowledge base.
Every rule is testable by an AI agent — no human judgment required.
Every rule is domain-agnostic — works for any topic.


1. Foundation
Rules for bootstrapping a new knowledge base.
Rule: Every vault must have a root note (_root.md) that states the vault’s purpose in one sentence.
Why: Without a clear purpose, notes drift aimlessly and the graph lacks coherence.
Test: Does _root.md exist? Can you extract a single-sentence purpose statement from it?
Rule: Establish 3 foundational convention notes before adding content — covering atomicity, linking, and navigation.
Why: Conventions defined early prevent inconsistency; retrofitting is expensive.
Test: Do convention notes exist? Do the 10 most recent notes reference or follow them?
Rule: Include a machine-readable config file (.mcp.json or equivalent) for AI tooling.
Why: AI agents need graph navigation tools to evaluate and improve the vault programmatically.
Test: Does the config file exist? Can an AI agent query the graph structure?
Rule: Use flat file structure with wikilinks — no nested folders.
Why: Folders impose hierarchy that constrains discovery; links create organic, multi-dimensional structure.
Test: Are all notes in a single directory? Are connections made via [[wikilinks]], not folder paths?
Rule: Ignore generated files — never commit cache, logs, or build artifacts.
Why: Generated files bloat the repository and cause merge conflicts.
Test: Does .gitignore exclude cache directories, logs, and build output?

2. Atomicity
One idea per note, properly sized.
Rule: Every note must contain exactly one idea, summarizable in one sentence.
Why: Multi-idea notes are hard to link, hard to find, and impossible to reuse in new contexts.
Test: Can the note be summarized in one sentence without losing its core meaning? If the summary requires “and”, split it.
Rule: Notes should be 100-300 words. Under 100 is too thin; over 300 likely contains multiple ideas.
Why: Size is a reliable proxy for atomicity — oversized notes almost always bundle concepts.
Test: Count words. Flag notes outside the 100-300 range for review.
Rule: When splitting a note, create bidirectional links between the parts and rewire all incoming links.
Why: Splitting without rewiring breaks navigation paths and orphans content.
Test: After a split, do both new notes link to each other? Do all previous incoming links point to the correct part?
Rule: Each note must be self-contained — readable without clicking any links.
Why: Notes that depend on external context become meaningless when links break or context shifts.
Test: Read the note in isolation. Does it make sense without following any links?

3. Linking
Meaningful connections between notes.
Rule: Every note must link to at least 2 other notes.
Why: Unlinked notes are invisible in the graph — they cannot be discovered through navigation.
Test: Run an orphan scan. Flag any note with fewer than 2 outgoing links.
Rule: Every link must be explainable in one sentence — no decorative or keyword-match links.
Why: Meaningless links inflate the graph without adding navigational or conceptual value.
Test: For each link in a note, can you state why the reader should follow it? Remove any link where you cannot.
Rule: Use diverse link types — relevance, contrast, analogy, cause/effect, category membership.
Why: Homogeneous links create a flat graph; diverse connection types enable richer traversal and insight.
Test: Categorize links in a random sample of 5 notes. Are at least 3 different link types represented across the sample?
Rule: Prefer linking to existing notes over creating new ones — search before you create.
Why: Duplicate notes fragment knowledge; linking to existing notes strengthens the graph.
Test: Before creating a note, search the vault. Does a note covering this idea already exist?

4. Navigation
The graph must be traversable and well-structured.
Rule: Every note must be reachable from _root.md in 3 hops or fewer.
Why: Deep chains indicate structural problems — important content becomes buried and undiscoverable.
Test: Run a breadth-first traversal from _root.md. Flag any note requiring more than 3 hops.
Rule: Create hub notes when a topic has 5+ related notes without a central entry point.
Why: Hubs provide navigation shortcuts, reduce traversal depth, and serve as orientation landmarks.
Test: Identify clusters of 5+ notes on a topic. Does each cluster have a hub note linking to all members?
Rule: Hub notes should link outward (to topic notes), not contain substantial content themselves.
Why: Hubs that contain long content violate atomicity; their job is navigation, not knowledge.
Test: Is the hub note under 200 words? Does it primarily consist of links with brief context?
Rule: Graph density (edges/nodes ratio) should increase over time, not just node count.
Why: A vault that grows in notes but not connections is accumulating, not building knowledge.
Test: Compare edges/nodes ratio across time. Is it trending upward?

5. Quality Maintenance
Keeping the vault healthy over time.
Rule: Every note should have a last-reviewed date, updated on each review.
Why: Without review tracking, stale notes accumulate undetected.
Test: Do all notes have a last-reviewed field? Flag notes not reviewed in the last 30 days.
Rule: Never delete notes — deprecate them with a marker and a pointer to the replacement.
Why: Deletion breaks incoming links silently; deprecation preserves the graph while signaling staleness.
Test: Search for broken wikilinks. Are there any dead references? If yes, a deletion happened without cleanup.
Rule: Terminology must be consistent across the vault — pick one term per concept and use it everywhere.
Why: Inconsistent terminology fragments search results and confuses navigation.
Test: Search for synonym pairs (e.g., “note”/“node”, “link”/“connection”). Does the vault use one term consistently?
Rule: Run structural health checks regularly — orphan scan, broken link scan, hub distribution, oversized note scan.
Why: Problems accumulate silently; periodic scans catch issues before they compound.
Test: Can you run these 4 scans right now? Do they all pass?
Rule: Every note must use consistent formatting — same heading style, same link syntax, same frontmatter fields.
Why: Inconsistent formatting breaks tooling and makes automated processing unreliable.
Test: Sample 10 random notes. Do they all use the same heading levels, link format, and frontmatter structure?

6. Knowledge Processing
How new knowledge enters and integrates into the vault.
Rule: Capture first, process second — never try to write a perfect note on the first pass.
Why: Perfectionism at capture time slows input and loses ideas; separate capture from refinement.
Test: Is there an inbox or staging area? Do notes go through at least two passes before being considered complete?
Rule: Process incoming knowledge into atomic notes within 48 hours.
Why: Unprocessed captures degrade in value as context fades; a growing inbox signals a broken pipeline.
Test: Count unprocessed captures. Are there more than 10? If yes, the processing pipeline is stalled.
Rule: When integrating new knowledge, search for existing related notes first — extend, don’t duplicate.
Why: Creating a new note when a related one exists fragments the knowledge graph.
Test: For the last 5 notes added, was a search performed first? Could any of them have been merged into existing notes?
Rule: Write in your own words — do not copy-paste from sources.
Why: Copy-pasted content lacks the synthesis that creates genuine understanding and unique value.
Test: Compare note text against its source. Is the wording substantially different? Does it add interpretation?
Rule: Cite sources — every factual claim should trace back to an origin.
Why: Unsourced claims cannot be verified or updated when the underlying knowledge changes.
Test: Do factual notes include source references? Can each claim be traced to its origin?

7. Evolution
How notes and structure improve over time.
Rule: Notes evolve through lifecycle stages: draft → active → evergreen → deprecated.
Why: Not all notes are equal; lifecycle tracking focuses review effort on notes that matter.
Test: Can each note be assigned a lifecycle stage? Are deprecated notes marked clearly?
Rule: Iterate on existing notes rather than creating new ones on the same topic.
Why: Parallel notes on the same idea fragment knowledge; iteration concentrates and deepens it.
Test: Search for notes with similar titles or overlapping content. Should any be merged?
Rule: When understanding changes, update the note — don’t create a “v2”.
Why: Versioned duplicates confuse navigation; a single evolving note is the source of truth.
Test: Search for notes with version indicators (v2, “updated”, “new”). Merge them into the canonical note.
Rule: Review the most-linked notes first — they have the highest impact on vault quality.
Why: A flawed hub note propagates its problems to every note that links through it.
Test: Run graph_hubs. Have the top 5 hubs been reviewed in the last 2 weeks?

8. Domain Agnosticism
Rules that ensure portability across any topic.
Rule: Every quality criterion must apply to any knowledge domain — software, cooking, history, physics.
Why: Domain-specific rules limit reuse; the Seed must work everywhere.
Test: For each rule, substitute 3 different domains. Does the rule still make sense?
Rule: Structure is universal; only content is domain-specific.
Why: Atomicity, linking, and navigation work identically regardless of subject matter.
Test: Could you replace all note content with a different domain’s content and have the structure still be valid?
Rule: Hub notes should reflect the domain’s natural concept hierarchy, not an imposed taxonomy.
Why: Forcing a generic taxonomy onto domain content creates friction; let the domain’s own structure emerge.
Test: Do hub note titles match how practitioners in the domain naturally categorize knowledge?

9. Self-Improvement
How the vault and its rules continuously improve.
Rule: The vault must follow its own rules — self-referential accuracy is the ultimate test.
Why: A knowledge base about quality criteria that violates its own criteria has no credibility.
Test: Pick 5 random notes. Do they all pass every rule in this Seed? If not, fix the notes or fix the rules.
Rule: Every rule in the Seed must be demonstrated by at least one note in the vault.
Why: Untested rules are theories; only rules proven in practice belong in the Seed.
Test: For each rule, can you point to a note that exemplifies it? Remove rules that have no evidence.
Rule: If the vault consistently violates a rule, the rule may be wrong — update or remove it.
Why: Rules that don’t survive contact with reality are bad rules.
Test: Are there rules that are frequently violated? Investigate whether the rule or the vault is wrong.
Rule: The Seed should shrink over time, not grow — consolidate and eliminate redundancy.
Why: A bloated ruleset is hard to follow; fewer, stronger rules beat many weak ones.
Test: Are any two rules saying the same thing? Merge them. Is any rule a subset of another? Remove the subset.
Rule: Track quality metrics over time: note count, link density, orphan count, average note size, broken links.
Why: Without metrics, improvement is anecdotal; with metrics, it’s verifiable.
Test: Can you produce these 5 metrics right now? Are they trending in the right direction?

AI-Assisted-Knowledge-Management.title: AI-Assisted Knowledge Management
AI-Assisted-Knowledge-Management.content:
AI-Assisted Knowledge Management
Using AI to enhance your knowledge base without losing ownership.
The Synergy
AI + Knowledge Base = Powerful combination:

























Without AIWith AIManual note creationAI assists draftingSelf-linkingAI suggests connectionsManual searchAI understands contextSlow iterationContinuous improvement

How AI Helps
1. Note Creation

AI helps distill long content into atomic notes
Generates first drafts you refine
Suggests atomic structure

2. Linking

AI analyzes notes and suggests links
Finds hidden connections (2-hop relations)
Identifies hub nodes

3. Search & Synthesis

Natural language queries find relevant content
AI synthesizes across multiple notes
Answers questions from your knowledge base

4. Maintenance

AI runs health checks
Identifies anti-patterns
Suggests note splits and merges


AI Caveats
Don’t Lose Ownership

Your brain, not AI’s - You are the thinker
Process over output - Writing is thinking
Verify, don’t trust - AI can hallucinate

Quality Control

AI suggestions are starting points, not gospel
Always verify sources
Maintain link quality


Implementation
Our vault uses AI via heartbeat:

AI explores - Generates new note drafts
AI audits - Finds violations
AI connects - Suggests links
Human decides - You approve all changes

This is exactly what we’re doing right now!

AI-Assisted Note Splitting
AI can identify and split oversized notes into atomic units.
Capability
AI systems can:

Detect notes exceeding word count thresholds (200-300 words)
Identify multiple ideas within a single note
Create split notes with appropriate content
Preserve and rewire links bidirectionally

Quality Criteria
Test: Can AI split notes while maintaining graph integrity?

Check: Are resulting notes truly atomic (one idea each)?
Check: Are all original links preserved appropriately?
Check: Are bidirectional links created where meaningful?

Splitting Triggers





















TriggerThresholdWord count> 300 wordsMultiple ideas> 1 H2/H3 headingConcept density> 3 distinct concepts
Process

Identify — AI finds oversized/overfull notes
Analyze — AI identifies natural split points
Create — AI drafts new atomic notes
Link — AI creates bidirectional links
Verify — Human reviews split quality

AI-Assisted-Orphan-Detection.title: AI-Assisted Orphan Detection
AI-Assisted-Orphan-Detection.content:
AI-Assisted Orphan Detection
AI can automatically detect orphaned notes in a knowledge base.
Capability
AI systems can identify notes that:

Have no incoming links (orphans)
Have no outgoing links (isolates)
Are unreachable from root in ≤3 hops

Quality Criteria
Test: Is every note reachable and connected?

Check: Run graph analysis to find notes with 0 incoming links
Check: Verify all notes are reachable from _root.md
Check: Identify notes with < 2 total connections

Why Orphans Matter

Orphans indicate gaps in knowledge structure
Unlinked notes are effectively invisible in the graph
They signal either: new content not yet connected, or content that doesn’t fit

Remediation Process

























StepAction1AI identifies orphan candidates2Human reviews each orphan3Decide: connect, merge, or remove4Update graph structure

Active-Reading.title: Active Reading
Active-Reading.content:
Active Reading
Active reading means engaging deeply with knowledge base content rather than passively consuming it.
The Three Pillars
1. Think About the Material
When reading notes, constantly ask:

Does this connect to something I already know?
What questions does this raise?
How can I apply this knowledge?

2. Bring in New Thoughts
Ideas emerge from engagement. Capture:

Questions that arise
Contradictions you notice
Extensions or applications
Personal associations

3. Add New Notes
Immediately create atomic notes from your thoughts:

Don’t trust memory — write it down
Each thought = one atomic note
Link to where the thought originated

Why It Matters
The second brain only grows when you actively contribute. Reading without thinking produces no new knowledge. Every note added is a connection made.

Linking Principle

Anti-Patterns-in-Knowledge-Management.title: Anti-Patterns in Knowledge Management
Anti-Patterns-in-Knowledge-Management.content:
Anti-Patterns in Knowledge Management
Common mistakes that undermine a second brain’s effectiveness.
1. Note Hoarding
Problem: Saving everything without processing.
Signs:

Hundreds of unprocessed bookmarks
Notes that are just copied text
No links between notes

Solution: Only keep what you process into atomic notes.

2. Over-Organization
Problem: Spending more time organizing than learning.
Signs:

Perfect folder hierarchies
Complex tagging systems
No actual notes being created

Solution: Let structure emerge from links, not folders.

3. Link Inflation
Problem: Linking everything to everything.
Signs:

Every note links to 10+ others
Links without context (“see also”)
No meaningful connections

Solution: Follow quality over quantity.

4. Perfectionism
Problem: Never publishing a note because it’s “not ready.”
Signs:

No new notes for weeks
Notes constantly being revised
No iteration

Solution: Write for your future self, not for perfection. Use iterate later.

5. Single-Source Dependency
Problem: One note depends entirely on one source.
Signs:

No sources cited
Notes that don’t make sense without the original
Can’t verify claims

Solution: Always add sources and write independently.

6. Neglecting Maintenance
Problem: Only adding, never reviewing.
Signs:

No health checks
Broken links accumulate
Outdated information

Solution: Run regular audits per iteration cycle.

7. Tool Overfocus
Problem: Obsessing over the perfect tool.
Signs:

Switching apps constantly
No actual knowledge captured
Setup takes priority over use

Solution: Use principles over tools.

Related

Atomic Note Principle
Link Quality Rule
Graph Maintenance
Self-Improvement Cycle

Atomic-Note-Principle.title: Atomic Note Principle
Atomic-Note-Principle.content:
Atomic Note Principle
A note must be atomic — it contains exactly one single idea.
Rule

Each note should focus on one concept, idea, or piece of knowledge
If a note grows beyond its original scope, it should be split into separate atomic notes
The maximum size for a note is approximately 200-300 words or one screen’s worth of content

Test Criteria (for AI Evaluation)

 Can you summarize the note in one sentence?
 Does the note cover only one idea?
 Is the note ≤300 words?
 Could this note be reused in a different context?

Why It Matters

Atomic notes are reusable across different contexts
Smaller notes are easier to link and connect
Graph traversal becomes more efficient with atomic units

Examples

✅ Good: “Zettelkasten Method explains the core principle of atomic notes”
❌ Bad: “Zettelkasten Method and how to implement it and tools and examples”

Related

Note Splitting Principle
Link Quality Rule
Graph Traversal Efficiency

CLAUDE.title: CLAUDE
CLAUDE.content:
CLAUDE.md
Dies ist ein Obsidian Vault nach Zettelkasten-Prinzipien.
Bevorzugte Sprache

Deutsch ist die bevorzugte Sprache für alle Notizen und Kommunikation in diesem Vault
Englische Fachbegriffe werden nicht übersetzt - etablierte Trading- und Finanzbegriffe bleiben auf Englisch (z.B. Moving Average, Support and Resistance, Price Action, Golden Cross)
Englische Fachbegriffe in Dateinamen sind gewünscht - z.B. “Break of Structure.md” statt “Bruch der Struktur.md”, “Fair Value Gap.md” statt “Faire Wert Lücke.md”
Code ist immer auf Englisch - Variablen, Funktionen, Kommentare in Code-Blöcken

Format & Tools

Markdown - All content is written in Markdown format
Obsidian Links - Use Wikilinks to connect notes (e.g., [[Concept Name]])
MCP Servers - This vault uses vault-graph and vault-sources MCP servers for navigation and management
Mermaid Diagrams - Mermaid diagrams are highly appreciated when they add visual clarity to a concept (e.g., flowcharts, mind maps, graphs)

Themengebiete

Trading-Strategien
Technische Analyse
Algorithmischer Handel
Day Trading
Swing Trading
Investieren
Finanzen und Wirtschaft

Motivation

Trading allgemein verstehen lernen
Profitable Ansätze im algorithmischen Handel finden
Profitable Ansätze im manuellen Trading finden
Grundlagen von Finanzen und Wirtschaft verstehen

Hintergrund

Ich bin Softwareentwickler - allgemeine Software-Engineering-Themen sind nicht relevant
Dieser Vault konzentriert sich auf Trading-Wissen, nicht auf Programmier-Grundlagen
Ausnahme: Trading-spezifische Tools, Bibliotheken und Frameworks sind relevant (z.B. Backtesting-Frameworks, Trading-APIs, quantitative Bibliotheken)

Kernprinzipien
Atomarität

“Put things which belong together in a Zettel, but try to separate concerns from one another”
Jede Notiz fokussiert sich auf eine Idee - das maximiert die Wiederverwendbarkeit im Netzwerk
Atomare Notizen fördern Verbindungen: “Creativity is just connecting things”

Vernetzung vor Hierarchie

Links sind wichtiger als der Speicherort - die Position ist irrelevant, wenn man eine Notiz von überall referenzieren kann
Prinzipien erzeugen Techniken: Fokus auf das Verbinden von Ideen, nicht auf ein perfektes Ablagesystem
Lass Struktur organisch durch Links entstehen, nicht durch starre Ordnerhierarchien
Neue Notizen immer verknüpfen - beim Erstellen einer neuen Seite den connect-notes Skill nutzen, um über den vault-graph MCP Server verwandte Notizen zu finden und sinnvolle Links zu setzen
Links nur wenn inhaltlich begründet - nicht jede thematische Nähe rechtfertigt einen Link. Qualität vor Quantität
Bidirektionale Links sind optional - Obsidian bietet Backlinks zur Rückwärts-Navigation. Bestehende Notizen nur dann aktualisieren, wenn der Link den Lesefluss oder das Verständnis dieser Notiz verbessert (z.B. als Beispiel, Anwendungsfall oder wichtige Ergänzung)

graph LR
    subgraph Bestehend
        A[Moving Averages]
        B[Technical Indicators]
        C[Trend Following]
    end

    subgraph Neu
        D[EMA Crossover Strategy]
    end

    D -->|verwendet| A
    D -->|ist ein| B
    D -->|basiert auf| C
    A -.->|optional: siehe auch| D
    C -.->|optional: Beispiel| D

Drei-Schichten-Struktur

Unten: Einzelne atomare Notizen (die Bausteine)
Mitte: Strukturnotizen, die verwandte Inhalte organisieren (wie MOCs - Maps of Content)
Oben: Haupt-Strukturnotizen und Index-Tags zur Navigation

Schreibe für dein zukünftiges Ich

Schreibe so, dass du es später verstehst - verwende klare, interpretierende Sprache
Sammle nicht nur Zitate; setze dich aktiv mit Quellen auseinander und nutze deine eigene Analyse
Eine Notiz sollte eigenständig verständlich sein, ohne die Originalquelle zu benötigen

Quellen

Füge immer Quellen hinzu, wenn möglich - zitiere, woher die Information stammt
Füge Quellen in einem dedizierten ## Quellen-Abschnitt am Ende jeder Notiz hinzu
Verwende URLs, Buchverweise, Paper-Zitate oder Links zu anderen Notizen als Quellen
Quellen ermöglichen Verifizierung und weitere Erkundung des Themas

Tags
Neben #notiz (Pflicht für alle atomaren Notizen) und #moc (für Strukturnotizen) verwenden wir thematische Tags zur losen Kategorisierung:

















































TagBeschreibungnotizPflicht-Tag für alle atomaren NotizenmocStrukturnotizen (Maps of Content)strategieTrading-Strategien (Entry/Exit-Systeme, Backtests)indikatorTechnische Indikatoren (RSI, MACD, ATR, Bollinger, etc.)psychologieTrading-Psychologie, Mindset, DisziplinderivateOptionen, CFDs, Knock-outs, Futures, VolatilitätrisikoRisk Management, Position Sizing, Backtesting-ValidierungorderflowOrderflow, Volumen-Trading, Footprint, DOMmlMachine Learning, NLP, Reinforcement Learning im TradinggrundlagenBörsen-Basics, Handelsplätze, Orderarten, Tools
Regeln

Tags ergänzen, nicht ersetzen — #notiz bleibt immer
Jede Notiz bekommt 1–3 thematische Tags zusätzlich
MOCs behalten nur #moc (keine thematischen Tags)
Alle Tags lowercase, keine Umlaute (ae statt ä)
Bestehende Tags wie youtube oder algotrading bleiben erhalten

Interne Link-Syntax
[[Notizname]]                    # Einfacher Wikilink
[[Notizname|Anzeigetext]]        # Link mit benutzerdefiniertem Anzeigetext
[[Notizname#überschrift|Überschrift]]        # Link zu bestimmter Überschrift
[[Notizname#^block-id|^block-id]]          # Link zu bestimmtem Block

# Moving Averages
 
Ein [[Technical Indicator]], der verwendet wird, um [[Price Action]] über Zeit zu glätten.
 
## Arten
 
- [[Simple Moving Average|SMA]] - gleiche Gewichtung aller Perioden
- [[Exponential Moving Average|EMA]] - höhere Gewichtung aktueller Preise
 
## Häufige Anwendungen
 
- Identifizierung der [[Trend Direction]]
- Finden von [[Support and Resistance]]-Niveaus
- Generierung von [[Crossover Signals]]
 
## Verwandt
 
- [[MACD]] verwendet EMAs zur Messung des Momentums
- [[Bollinger Bands]] basieren auf SMAs
- Siehe auch: [[Moving Average Strategies#golden-cross|Golden Cross]]
 
## Quellen
 
- Murphy, J. (1999). *Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets*
- www.investopedia.com/terms/m/movingaverage.asp
Beispiel: Strukturnotiz (MOC)
Strukturnotizen organisieren Cluster verwandter atomarer Notizen. Sie befinden sich in der “mittleren Schicht.”
# Technical Analysis MOC
 
Einstiegspunkt für alle Technical Analysis-Konzepte in diesem Vault.
 
## Price-Based Indicators
- [[Moving Averages]] - Trendglättung
- [[Bollinger Bands]] - Volatilitätskanäle
- [[Support and Resistance]] - wichtige Preisniveaus
 
## Momentum Indicators
- [[RSI]] - Overbought/Oversold
- [[MACD]] - Trend-Momentum
- [[Stochastic Oscillator]] - Momentum-Extreme
 
## Volume Analysis
- [[Volume Profile]]
- [[On-Balance Volume]]
 
## Chart Patterns
- [[Head and Shoulders]]
- [[Double Top and Bottom]]
- [[Flags and Pennants]]
 
---
Siehe auch: [[Trading-Strategien MOC]] | [[Risk Management MOC]]
Verarbeitungs-Workflow
Beim Hinzufügen von neuem Wissen zum Vault:

Erfassen - Rohe Notizen, Zitate, Ideen in einem Posteingang sammeln
Clustern - Notizen nach Thema gruppieren, nicht nach Quelle
Schreiben - Atomare Notizen aus Clustern erstellen, in eigenen Worten
Verbinden - Neue Notizen mit bestehenden verlinken; Strukturnotizen aktualisieren

Mermaid-Diagramme
Mermaid-Diagramme sind sehr willkommen zur Visualisierung von:

Trading-Strategien und Entscheidungsbäumen
Systemarchitekturen (z.B. Backtesting-Pipelines)
Abläufen und Workflows
Beziehungen zwischen Konzepten

Validierung
Vor dem Einfügen von Mermaid-Diagrammen sollte deren Syntax validiert werden:
# Einzelne Datei validieren
./scripts/validate-mermaid.sh diagram.mmd
 
# Alle Mermaid-Blöcke in einer Markdown-Datei validieren
./scripts/validate-mermaid.sh note.md
 
# Direkt aus stdin validieren
echo "graph TD; A-->B" | ./scripts/validate-mermaid.sh
Beispiel
graph TD
    A[Signal erkannt] --> B{Bestätigung?}
    B -->|Ja| C[Entry]
    B -->|Nein| D[Warten]
    C --> E[Stop Loss setzen]
    C --> F[Take Profit setzen]

Richtlinien

Verwende [[doppelte Klammern]] für alle internen Referenzen
Erstelle Links auch wenn die Zielnotiz noch nicht existiert (Obsidian zeigt sie als unaufgelöst an)
Bevorzuge beschreibenden Linktext: [[EMA|Exponential Moving Average]] liest sich besser im Fließtext
Füge einen “Verwandt” oder “Siehe auch”-Abschnitt hinzu, um Konzepte explizit zu verbinden
Halte Techniken minimal - Einfachheit vor Komplexität
Tags für lose Kategorisierung, Links für bedeutungsvolle Verbindungen
Jede neue Notiz bekommt das Tag #notiz im Frontmatter (tags: [notiz]). MOCs verwenden stattdessen #moc.

Skills
connect-notes
Der Skill .claude/skills/connect-notes/ nutzt den vault-graph MCP Server, um beim Erstellen oder Bearbeiten von Notizen passende Verbindungen im Vault zu finden. Er wird automatisch geladen, wenn Notizen erstellt oder bearbeitet werden, und kann auch manuell mit /connect-notes aufgerufen werden.
Funktionen:

Findet thematisch verwandte Notizen über Graph-Suche und Nachbarschafts-Analyse
Identifiziert zentrale Hub-Notizen, an die neue Inhalte angebunden werden sollten
Entdeckt nicht-offensichtliche Verbindungen über 2-Hop-Relationen
Setzt Links nach den Vault-Richtlinien (sinnvoll, nicht inflationär, bidirektional nur bei Mehrwert)

delete-note
Der Skill .claude/skills/delete-note/ loescht eine Markdown-Datei aus dem Vault und bereinigt alle eingehenden Links. Er nutzt den vault-graph MCP Server, um Referenzen zu finden, und den vault-sources MCP Server, um die Provenance-DB zu aktualisieren. Manuell aufrufbar mit /delete-note.
Funktionen:

Ermittelt alle eingehenden Links über Graph-Analyse
Bereinigt Links kontextabhängig (MOC-Einträge entfernen, Fließtext-Links zu Plaintext)
Löscht die Datei und aktualisiert die Provenance-DB
Verifiziert, dass keine verwaisten Links oder neue Orphans entstanden sind

navigate-vault
Der Skill .claude/skills/navigate-vault/ nutzt den vault-graph MCP Server, um durch das Wissensnetzwerk zu navigieren. Er wird verwendet, wenn Notizen gesucht, Zusammenhänge erkundet oder Inhalte über Links und Backlinks gefunden werden sollen. Manuell aufrufbar mit /navigate-vault.
Funktionen:

Springt von Notiz zu Notiz über ausgehende Links (vorwärts) und Backlinks (rückwärts)
Erkundet Themenbereiche über Hubs und Nachbarschafts-Ranking
Findet Zusammenhänge zwischen zwei Konzepten über gemeinsame Nachbarn
Identifiziert verwaiste Notizen, die noch nicht im Graph eingebunden sind

Provenance-Tracking (vault-sources MCP)
Der vault-sources MCP Server ist ein Provenance-Ledger, das nachverfolgt, welche Eingabequellen (Transkripte, Buchkapitel, Artikel) welche Notizen im Vault erzeugt haben. Er speichert keine Daten im Vault selbst, sondern in einer separaten SQLite-Datenbank.
Workflow: Neue Notiz mit Provenance erstellen

Quelle speichern: store_input mit dem Rohtext aufrufen (Transkript, Buchkapitel, etc.) — gibt eine input_id zurück
Notiz-ID generieren: generate_note_id aufrufen — gibt eine UUIDv7 zurück
Markdown-Datei schreiben mit der ID im Frontmatter:
---
vault_sources_mcp_id: <UUIDv7>
tags:
  - notiz
---

Notiz registrieren: register_note mit der note_id aufrufen
Quelle verknüpfen: add_link mit input_id und note_id aufrufen
Vernetzung: connect-notes Skill nutzen, um Wikilinks zu setzen

Workflow: Provenance einer Notiz prüfen

vault_sources_mcp_id aus dem Frontmatter der Notiz lesen
get_sources_for_note mit der note_id aufrufen — zeigt alle verknüpften Quellen
get_input für eine bestimmte Quelle aufrufen — gibt den Originaltext zurück

Typische Quellentypen

























source_typeBeschreibungbook_chapterBuchkapitel (z.B. aus “Das große Trading Buch”)youtube_transcriptYouTube-Video-TranskriptarticleArtikel oder Blog-Postpasted_textManuell eingefügter Text
Verfügbare MCP Tools





























































ToolZweckdb_statusDatenbank-Status und Statistiken prüfendb_initDatenbank initialisieren (einmalig)store_inputRohtext speichern (SHA-256 Deduplizierung)get_inputGespeicherten Input abrufenlist_inputsAlle Inputs auflisten (Metadaten)generate_note_idUUIDv7 für neue Notiz generierenregister_noteNotiz in der Provenance-DB registrierenadd_linkInput mit Notiz verknüpfenget_sources_for_noteAlle Quellen einer Notiz findenget_notes_for_inputAlle Notizen einer Quelle findenfind_orphaned_inputsInputs ohne verknüpfte Notizen findenfind_unlinked_notesNotizen ohne bekannte Quellen findenfind_stale_notesLange nicht gesehene Notizen finden

Claude-Knowledge-Base-Setup.title: Claude Knowledge Base Setup
Claude-Knowledge-Base-Setup.content:
Claude Knowledge Base Setup

How to bootstrap an AI-friendly knowledge base for Claude, Codex, Gemini, and other AI agents.

Different AI agents use different configuration files. This guide helps you create a knowledge base that AI agents can navigate and maintain.

Agent Configuration Files






























AgentConfig FilePurposeClaudeCLAUDE.mdInstructions for Claude CodeCodexAGENTS.mdAgent behavior guidelinesGemini.gemini/rulesGemini instructionsOpenAI`system_instructions.md instructions
This` | Custom guide focuses on CLAUDE.md (Claude Code), but principles apply to all.

Required Files
1. CLAUDE.md
The main instruction file. Put in vault root.
# CLAUDE.md - [Vault Name]
 
## Purpose
[What this knowledge base covers]
 
## Structure
- Notes are atomic (one idea per file)
- Use [[Wikilinks]] to connect notes
- MCP servers: vault-graph, vault-sources
 
## Agent Guidelines
- Read before responding
- Follow [[Atomic Note Principle]]
- Use graph tools to navigate
- Make one logical change per commit
2. AGENTS.md (Optional)
For Codex or multi-agent setups.
# AGENTS.md
 
## Agent Behavior
- Always read CLAUDE.md first
- Follow vault principles
- Commit changes to "Neo" branch
3. MCP Configuration
.mcp.json for vault navigation:
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "vault-graph": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": ["/path/to/vault-graph-mcp/dist/index.js"],
      "env": { "VAULT_PATH": "/path/to/vault" }
    },
    "vault-sources": {
      "command": "node", 
      "args": ["/path/to/vault-sources-mcp/dist/src/index.js"],
      "env": { "VAULT_PATH": "/path/to/vault" }
    }
  }
}

The 3 Core Principles (For AI)
1. Atomic Notes
AI should create notes with one idea each.
2. Meaningful Links
Every note links to related notes using Wikilinks.
3. Graph Navigation
Use MCP tools to navigate:

graph_hubs — Find entry points
graph_search(query) — Find notes
graph_get_neighbors(note) — Explore connections


Agent Workflow
When AI works in this vault:
1. Read CLAUDE.md for instructions
2. Pull latest: git pull origin main
3. Navigate: graph_search, graph_hubs
4. Read relevant notes
5. Make one atomic change
6. Commit: git add -A && git commit -m "[Type] What"
7. Push: git push origin main


File Naming

Use kebab-case.md or CamelCase.md
Spaces in titles become hyphens in filenames
Example: Atomic Note Principle.md


Git Workflow
AI should create a branch before changes:
git checkout -b Neo  # Create branch
# Make changes
git add -A
git commit -m "[Type] Description"
git push origin Neo

Quick Start

Create CLAUDE.md with vault purpose
2.root.mdas Add_ entry point
Add 3 foundational notes:

Atomic Note Principle
Linking Principle
Graph Traversal Efficiency


Configure MCP servers
Add to Git and push

Common-Knowledge-Base-Mistakes.title: Common Knowledge Base Mistakes
Common-Knowledge-Base-Mistakes.content:
Common Knowledge Base Mistakes
Errors that derail your knowledge management system.
Starting Too Complex
Mistake
Setting up elaborate templates, folders, tagging systems before writing any notes.
Solution
Start simple. Just write notes and link them.

Obsessing Over Tools
Mistake
Switching apps, trying plugins, customizing themes instead of capturing knowledge.
Solution
Tools serve knowledge, not the reverse.

Creating Orphan Notes
Mistake
Writing notes without linking them to anything.
Solution
Every note should link to 2-3 related notes. Run graph_isolated_nodes weekly.

Over-Linking
Mistake
Linking every note to everything, creating a messy graph.
Solution
Quality over quantity. Only link when there’s meaningful connection.

Skipping the Inbox
Mistake
Trying to fully process notes at capture time.
Solution
Capture quickly → Process later. Use an inbox.

Not Reviewing
Mistake
Building notes but never reviewing them.
Solution
Per Self-Improvement Cycle, run weekly health checks.

Related

Compound-Knowledge-Effects.title: Compound Knowledge Effects
Compound-Knowledge-Effects.content:
Compound Knowledge Effects
How small knowledge gains compound over time.
The Compound Equation
Knowledge builds on knowledge:
Day 1: 1 note → 1 connection
Day 30: 30 notes → 90 connections (compound!)
Day 100: 100 notes → 500+ connections (emergence!)

Why It Compounds
1. Network Effects
Each new note connects to existing notes:

1 note: 0-2 connections
10 notes: ~15 connections
50 notes: ~200 connections

2. Synthesis
Combining existing notes creates new insights:

Note A + Note B → Insight C
This is Emergence in Knowledge Graphs

3. Faster Processing
More context = faster note creation:

First note: 15 minutes
50th note: 5 minutes
100th note: 2 minutes

The Tipping Point
Around 50-100 notes, something shifts:

Notes start connecting organically
Insights emerge without forcing
System becomes self-sustaining

Implications

Start small - 1 note/day compounds
Be patient - Effects take time
Don’t skip days - Consistency matters
Trust the process - Compound effects are real

Real Numbers (Our Vault)

Cycle 1: 19 nodes
Cycle 13: 47 nodes
Connections grew: 95 → 274 (2.9x growth!)

Knowledge Retention

Daily-Review-Practice.title: Daily Review Practice
Daily-Review-Practice.content:
Daily Review Practice
Making review a habit for better knowledge retention.
Why Daily Review
Daily review reinforces Knowledge Retention:

Spaced repetition through regular engagement
Updates outdated information
Creates new connections

The 10-Minute Rule
10 minutes daily > 1 hour weekly:





















FrequencyEffectivenessDaily 10 minHigh retentionWeekly 1 hourMedium retentionMonthlyLow retention
Review Protocol
Morning (5 min)

Check inbox - process new captures
Review 3 random notes
Add 1 new link if possible

Evening (5 min)

Add new notes from today
Check for gaps in recent notes
Update any stale notes

What to Review

Recent notes (last 7 days)
High-value hubs
Isolated notes
Notes with broken links

Tracking
Use frontmatter to track reviews:
---
last-reviewed: 2026-03-07
review-count: 42
---

Emergence-in-Knowledge-Graphs.title: Emergence in Knowledge Graphs
Emergence-in-Knowledge-Graphs.content:
Emergence in Knowledge Graphs
Emergence occurs when a system exhibits properties its individual components don’t have alone.
In Knowledge Graphs
Simple components:

Individual atomic notes (one idea each)

Emergent properties:

New insights from note combinations
Unexpected connections between domains
Novel concepts arising from intersections

How Emergence Works
graph LR
    A[Note A: Concept 1] -->|intersection| C[Emergent Insight]
    B[Note B: Concept 2] -->|intersection| C
    C -->|becomes| D[New Atomic Note]


Two unrelated notes share hidden connection
Agent identifies intersection
New note created capturing emergent insight
Graph density increases, more emergence possible

Requirements for Emergence

Density - Enough notes and links for intersections
Diversity - Notes from different domains
Active linking - Regular connection creation
Agent attention - Identifying hidden patterns

Our Vault
We track emergence through:

graph_related - Find 2-hop connections
graph_get_neighbors_ranked - Identify strong intersections
Regular review in REFLECT mode

Anti-Patterns That Prevent Emergence

Too few notes (no critical mass)
Over-linking (everything connects to everything = noise)
No cross-domain notes
Agent only adds, never synthesizes

Evergreen-Notes.title: Evergreen Notes
Evergreen-Notes.content:
Evergreen Notes
Evergreen notes are notes that are continuously improved and expanded over time, unlike static notes that become outdated.
Concept (Andy Matuschak)
Key principles:

Write as if for your future self
Notes should be self-contained and understandable alone
Iterate on notes rather than abandoning them
Link forward to developing ideas

Lifecycle
Seedling → Bud → Evergreen
   ↓         ↓         ↓
  Raw     Developing  Mature
 idea     concept    knowledge

In Practice

Start with a seed note (small, focused)
Expand when new connections emerge
Merge similar notes to reduce fragmentation
Update outdated information on review

Connection to Other Notes

Knowledge Retention - evergreen notes improve long-term recall
Atomic Note Principle - atomicity enables recombination
Spaced Repetition - periodic review maintains quality

Exportable-Rules.title: Exportable Rules
Exportable-Rules.content:
Exportable Rules

Purpose: Drop these rules into any CLAUDE.md to bootstrap a high-quality, AI-maintained knowledge graph on any topic.

This document is auto-maintained by the heartbeat DISTILL mode. Every rule here is demonstrated by at least one note in this vault.

Quick Reference
Start Here

Exportable Rules - Structure - Atomic notes, splitting, note types
Exportable Rules - Linking - How to connect notes meaningfully
Exportable Rules - Graph Navigation - Finding your way around
Exportable Rules - Maintenance - Keeping the vault healthy

Core Principles

Atomic: One idea per note, 200-300 words max
Linked: 2-3 connections per note minimum
Hubbed: 3-hop maximum to any node
Maintained: Regular health checks, updates

Anti-Patterns to Avoid

Note Hoarding - Saving everything without processing
Over-Organization - Spending more time organizing than learning
Link Inflation - Linking everything to everything
Perfectionism - Never publishing because “not ready”
Single-Source Dependency - Note can’t stand alone
Neglecting Maintenance - Only adding, never reviewing

See Anti-Patterns in Knowledge Management for solutions.

The Pipeline
Input → Capture → Process → Connect → Maintain

Input - Collect from books, articles, videos, conversations
Capture - Quick save to inbox, add source reference
Process - Extract key points, write in your own words, split into atomic notes
Connect - Add 2-3 meaningful links to related notes
Maintain - Regular health checks, update outdated notes

See Second Brain Workflow for detailed daily practice.

Git-Best-Practices.title: Git Best Practices
Git-Best-Practices.content:
Git Best Practices for Second Brain
Using Git properly keeps the second brain clean and maintainable.
Rules
1. Atomic Commits
Each commit should represent one logical change:

✅ Good: “Add Zettelkasten Method node”
❌ Bad: “Self-improvement iteration 2”

2. Descriptive Messages
Commit messages should describe the knowledge added:
Add atomic node about [topic]
Add linking principle for [concept]
Refactor [node] into smaller atomic nodes

3. Ignore Cache Files
Never commit generated files:

.mcp-cache/ - Graph index cache
.obsidian/ - Editor settings
.vault-sources.sqlite - Provenance database
*.log - Log files

4. Iteration Log Strategy
The Second Brain Iteration Log should not be updated in every commit. Instead:

Only log significant improvements
Or keep the log locally and sync periodically

Commit Template
[Type] Brief description

- Added: [new node/link]
- Fixed: [issue]
- Improved: [change]

Types: Add, Fix, Refactor, Improve, Document
Related

Self-Improvement Cycle

Graph-Maintenance.title: Graph Maintenance
Graph-Maintenance.content:
Graph Maintenance
Regular maintenance keeps the knowledge base healthy and navigable.
Rule
Perform these checks regularly:

Isolated notes: Run graph_isolated_nodes — reconnect orphans
Link density: Ensure each note has 2-3+ links
Note size: Check for oversized notes — split if needed
Index health: Rebuild index with graph_build_index after major changes

Test Criteria (for AI Evaluation)

 Can you find all notes with < 2 links?
 Are all notes reachable from root in ≤3 hops?
 Is link density between 2-5 per note?
 Are there any notes > 300 words that need splitting?

Maintenance Checklist

 Check for isolated notes weekly
 Review note sizes monthly
 Verify link density on new notes
 Rebuild graph index after bulk additions

Self-Improvement Cycle
The knowledge base should evolve and improve itself:
Every 15 minutes:
1. Pull latest changes (git pull)
2. Read graph structure (graph_hubs, graph_search)
3. Review health (isolated notes, link density)
4. Fix issues found
5. Commit and push improvements

Agent Responsibilities

Monitor graph health proactively
Split notes that grow too large
Reconnect orphaned notes
Suggest new hub notes when needed
Document improvements in Graph Maintenance Log

Graph-Navigation-MCP.title: Graph Navigation MCP
Graph-Navigation-MCP.content:
Graph Navigation MCP

GitHub: github.com/pascalweiss/vault-graph-mcp

The vault-graph MCP server enables the agent to navigate and analyze the knowledge graph.
Available Tools
graph_search
Find nodes by title or keywords.
graph_search(query: "atomic", limit: 5)

graph_hubs
Find the most connected nodes (entry points).
graph_hubs(limit: 10)

graph_get_neighbors
Get direct connections from a node.
graph_get_neighbors(node: "Concept.md", direction: "both")

graph_get_neighbors_ranked
Get connections sorted by relevance.
graph_get_neighbors_ranked(node: "Concept.md", limit: 10)

graph_related
Find connections 2 hops away.
graph_related(node: "Concept.md", limit: 5)

graph_isolated_nodes
Find nodes without connections.
graph_isolated_nodes()

graph_build_index
Rebuild the graph index after changes.
graph_build_index(force: true)

Agent Navigation Workflow
flowchart TD
    A[New Query] --> B{Already in Graph?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Find via graph_search]
    B -->|No| D[Identify related concepts]
    D --> E[graph_related to find 2-hop connections]
    E --> F[graph_get_neighbors_ranked]
    F --> G[Read top results]
    G --> H[Synthesize answer]

Best Practices

Always rebuild index after bulk changes: graph_build_index
Use graph_hubs to find good entry points
Check graph_isolated_nodes regularly
Use graph_related for discovery

Related

Note Insertion Strategy
Graph Traversal Efficiency
Graph Maintenance

Graph-Traversal-Efficiency.title: Graph Traversal Efficiency
Graph-Traversal-Efficiency.content:
Graph Traversal Efficiency
The agent must be able to quickly navigate from any note to find requested information.
Rule

Maximum 3 hops to reach any note from a hub
Use hub notes as entry points for broad topics
Ensure every note is reachable from at least one hub

Test Criteria (for AI Evaluation)

 Can any note be reached in ≤3 hops from a hub?
 Do hub notes exist for major topics?
 Are there any isolated notes with <2 links?
 Can you navigate from root to any note in ≤3 steps?

Agent Navigation Strategy
1. Identify the query topic
2. Start from relevant hub note
3. Follow links through intermediate notes
4. Reach target note within 3 hops

Hub Nodes
Hub notes are highly connected entries for broad topics:

Use graph_hubs to find most connected notes
Create hub notes for major themes
Hub notes should link to sub-topic notes

Why 3 Hops?

Beyond 3 hops, relevance decreases rapidly
3 hops balance depth with discoverability
Agents can traverse efficiently

Maintenance
Regularly check:

graph_isolated_nodes — find orphaned notes
graph_related — find hidden connections
Rebuild index if graph feels slow

Related

Graph Maintenance

Graph-vs-Hierarchical-Filing.title: Graph vs Hierarchical Filing
Graph-vs-Hierarchical-Filing.content:
Graph vs Hierarchical Filing
Two fundamentally different approaches to organizing knowledge: network vs tree.
Hierarchical Filing
Knowledge/
├── Topics/
│   ├── Science/
│   │   ├── Physics/
│   │   └── Biology/
│   └── History/
│       ├── Ancient/
│       └── Modern/
└── Projects/

Characteristics:

Folder-based, location-dependent
One parent per item
Good for storage, bad for discovery

Knowledge Graph (Our Approach)
[[Atomic Note]] ←→ [[Related Concept]] ←→ [[Application]]
       ↓                  ↓                  ↓
   [[Example]]      [[Critique]]      [[Tool]]

Characteristics:

Link-based, location-independent
Multiple connections per node
Good for discovery, emergent structure

Comparison



































AspectHierarchyGraphNavigationBy locationBy associationDiscoveryHard (where is it?)Easy (what connects?)StructureImposedEmergentFlexibilityLowHighRedundancyDuplicate filesLink to same note
Why Graph Wins

No “where should I put this?” - Just link to related notes
Serendipitous discovery - Follow connections you didn’t know existed
No duplicate notes - One atomic note, many links
Living structure - Structure emerges from content, not rigid folders
Better for AI - Graph traversal mirrors reasoning

Hybrid Approach
Use hierarchy for broad categorization (PARA), graph for internal linking:
PARA/Resources/
├── Finance/
│   └── [All finance notes linked by graph]
└── Tech/
    └── [All tech notes linked by graph]

Handling-Outdated-Information.title: Handling Outdated Information
Handling-Outdated-Information.content:
Handling Outdated Information
Keeping your second brain accurate and current.
The Problem
Notes become outdated:

Technology changes
Understanding evolves
Facts become incorrect
Links break

Strategies
1. Version Tracking
Add metadata to track changes:
---
created: 2025-01-15
modified: 2026-03-07
status: [current|needs-update|deprecated]
---
2. Deprecation Markers
For outdated notes:
# Deprecated: [Old Concept]
 
> **Status:** This concept has been superseded by [[New Concept]]
 
**Reason:** [Brief explanation]
**Date:** 2026-03-07
3. Evergreen Updates
Per Evergreen Notes, iterate on notes rather than abandoning:

Update instead of creating new
Keep historical context
Link to updated versions

4. Regular Review
Per Graph Maintenance:

Quarterly review of old notes
Check for broken links
Verify accuracy

Red Flags

Notes with old dates
Contradictions between notes
Broken Wikilinks
Outdated tool recommendations

Best Practices

Date everything - Frontmatter with created/modified
Version your ideas - Don’t delete, deprecate
Review regularly - Self-Improvement Cycle
Trust but verify - Don’t assume notes are correct

Related

Evergreen Notes
Graph Maintenance
Self-Improvement Cycle

Hub-Node-Creation.title: Hub Node Creation
Hub-Node-Creation.content:
Hub Node Creation
Hub nodes are highly connected notes that serve as entry points for broad topics in the knowledge graph.
Purpose

Provide starting points for exploring a topic
Reduce navigation depth to any node
Create logical clusters of related knowledge

Characteristics

High connectivity (many incoming and outgoing links)
Broad topic coverage
Clear, descriptive titles
Links to sub-topic nodes

Creation Guidelines
When to Create

New broad topic emerges that isn’t covered
Multiple notes refer to a similar theme without a central hub
Topic grows beyond 5+ related notes

How to Create

Identify the common theme
Create a named note with clear title
Link to existing notes in the topic area
Add to relevant parent hubs

Example
# Technical Analysis
 
Entry point for all technical analysis concepts.
 
## Price-Based Indicators
- [[Moving Averages]]
- [[Bollinger Bands]]
- [[Support and Resistance]]
 
## Momentum Indicators
- [[RSI]]
- [[MACD]]
- [[Stochastic Oscillator]]
Maintenance

Review hub nodes monthly
Add new connections as knowledge grows
Split if hub becomes too broad

Ingestion-Mechanisms.title: Ingestion Mechanisms
Ingestion-Mechanisms.content:
Ingestion Mechanisms
How knowledge enters a knowledge base. Applies to human-only, AI-assisted, and autonomous systems.
The Problem
Knowledge is fleeting. Without mechanisms to capture it, insights are lost.
Ingestion Channels
1. Direct Input

Manual entry (human types)
Voice-to-text (human speaks, system transcribes)
API ingestion (AI pulls from external sources)

2. Document Processing

Web scraping
PDF/document parsing
Email ingestion
Feed parsing (RSS, Atom)

3. Media Capture

Screenshot tools
Image OCR
Audio transcription
Video frame extraction

4. System Integration

Webhook payloads
Database sync
Chatbot conversation logs
Browser extension captures

Quality Gate: Processing Window
Test: Is incoming knowledge processed within 48 hours?

Unprocessed content degrades knowledge base quality
AI systems can process faster (minutes vs days)
Human systems need dedicated review time

Recommended:

AI systems: process within hours
Human systems: process within 24-48 hours

Ingestion Quality Criteria

























CriterionTestSource preservationCan you trace back to original source?Timestamp accuracyIs capture time recorded?Processing timelinessProcessed within 48 hours?Atomic conversionRaw input → atomic notes?

Interleaving-and-Cross-Domain-Connection.title: Interleaving and Cross-Domain Connections
Interleaving-and-Cross-Domain-Connection.content:
Interleaving and Cross-Domain Connections
Mixing topics to create deeper understanding and unexpected insights.
The Problem with Block Learning
Most people study one topic at a time:
Math → Math → Math → Physics → Physics → Physics

This creates isolated knowledge silos.
Interleaving Solution
Mix topics during learning:
Math → Physics → Math → Chemistry → Physics → Math

Benefits

Better retention - Spacing improves long-term memory
Faster discrimination - Learn to distinguish concepts
Unexpected connections - Cross-domain insights emerge
Real-world application - Life doesn’t come in blocks

How It Works in Your Second Brain
1. Link Across Domains
When processing notes, actively seek connections to different fields:

Trading strategy → psychological concept
Programming pattern → biological system
Historical event → modern business

2. Create Bridge Notes
# The Lindy Effect

From: Aging software → Business longevity
Applies to: Trading strategies that work long-term
Connection: [[Risk Management]] principles

3. Use Tags for Interleaving
Tag notes with cross-domain indicators:

#analogy - Notes with cross-domain connections
#pattern - Reusable patterns across fields

Practical Application





















Instead ofDo ThisAll trading notes togetherMix with psychology, statisticsOnly coding notesLink to architecture, businessSingle-topic foldersCross-reference widely

Knowledge-Base-Essence.title: Knowledge Base Essence
Knowledge-Base-Essence.content:
Second Brain Essence

The minimal set of rules to bootstrap a healthy, self-improving knowledge base on any topic.

This document contains the distilled essence of how to build a second brain. Use it to initialize any new knowledge vault.

The 3 Core Principles
1. Atomic Notes
Every note contains one single idea. Maximum ~200-300 words.
✅ Good: "# Zettelkasten Method - core principles"
❌ Bad: Combined note about Zettelkasten AND tools AND examples

2. Meaningful Linking
Every note links to 2-3 related notes. Links must be content-driven.
[[Concept]] - brief explanation of how it connects
3. Graph Navigation
Maximum 3 hops to reach any note from a hub. Create hub notes as entry points.

The 5 Essential Rules



































RuleTriggerActionAtomicNote >300 wordsSplit into atomic notesLinkNew note addedLink to 2-3 related notesHubTopic growsCreate hub note as entry pointTraverseFinding infoMax 3 hops from hubHealthRegular checkFix isolated notes

The Maintenance Cycle
Run every 15 minutes (or daily for manual):
1. git pull
2. Check graph: graph_hubs, graph_isolated_nodes
3. Read 3-5 random notes
4. Fix violations (split, link, rewire)
5. git add + commit + push


The Bootstrap Process (Week 1)

































DayAction1Create _root.md (vault purpose)2Add Atomic Note Principle + Linking Principle3Process 1 document → 3-5 atomic notes4Link notes together5Run health check, fix issues6-7Repeat, aim for 10 notes
Target: 10 notes → 25 → 50 → 100

The MCP Tools
Use vault-graph MCP for navigation:

graph_hubs — Find entry points
graph_isolated_nodes — Find orphans
graph_search(query) — Find by keywords
graph_get_neighbors(note) — Get connections


The Anti-Patterns
Avoid:

Note hoarding (capture everything → process nothing)
Over-linking (links without meaning)
Skipping maintenance (only add, never fix)
Perfectionism (never publish because “not ready”)


Quick Start Template
# [Topic] Knowledge Base
 
## Purpose
[What does this vault cover?]
 
## Core Principles
- [[Atomic Note Principle]]
- [[Linking Principle]]
 
## Hub Notes
- [Topic] Hub → main entry point
 
## Daily Practice
- Capture → Process → Link → Review

Why This Works

Atomic → Reusable across contexts
Linked → Discoverable, navigable
Hub-based → 3-hop traversal
Maintained → Self-improving

The compound effect: more notes → more connections → more emergence → more value.

Knowledge-Base-Workflow.title: Knowledge Base Workflow
Knowledge-Base-Workflow.content:
Knowledge Management Workflow
A practical end-to-end workflow for building and maintaining a knowledge base. Works for human-only, AI-assisted, or fully autonomous systems.
The Full Pipeline
Input → Capture → Process → Connect → Maintain


1. Input (Ingestion)
Sources:

Books, articles, videos
Conversations and meetings
Ideas and observations
Research and experiments

For AI-assisted systems: Agents can autonomously capture from APIs, web searches, document processing, and more.
Tools:

Readwise, Instapaper (web articles)
Voice memos (voice-to-text)
Kindle highlights
Email clippings


2. Capture (Quick Save)
Save raw material without processing:

Use inbox/note to capture quickly
Add source URL/reference
Tag with broad category
Don’t organize yet — just capture


3. Process (The Key Step)
This is where Progressive Summarization meets Atomic Note Principle:

Review - Read through raw capture
Extract - Bold key points
Distill - Write in your own words
Split - Separate into atomic notes (one idea each)
Source - Add references

Time per note: 5-15 minutes max.

4. Connect (Linking)
After processing, link to existing knowledge:

Search: graph_search(keywords)
Find neighbors: graph_get_neighbors_ranked
Add 2-3 meaningful links per note
Update relevant MOCs if needed

See Note Insertion Strategy.

5. Maintain (Ongoing)
Per Self-Improvement Cycle:

Run graph health checks weekly
Review isolated nodes
Update outdated notes
Split overgrown notes


Daily Practice





















TimeActionMorningProcess 3-5 notes from inboxThroughoutCapture new inputs quicklyWeeklyRun graph maintenance

Tools We Use

Obsidian - Primary note-taking (graph-linked)
vault-graph MCP - Navigate and analyze
vault-sources MCP - Track provenance
AI agents - Can assist with capture, processing, linking, and maintenance

Knowledge-Retention.title: Knowledge Retention
Knowledge-Retention.content:
Knowledge Retention
Knowledge retention is the ability to maintain and recall information over time. In a second brain system, retention happens through two mechanisms:
Passive Retention

Well-linked notes are easier to find later
Graph structure surfaces related concepts
Search and backlinks enable rediscovery

Active Retention

Spaced Repetition for memorization
Writing notes in your own words (encoding)
Teaching concepts to others (elaboration)
Cross-domain connections (interleaving)

The forgetting Curve
Ebbinghaus discovered that without reinforcement, ~75% of new information is forgotten within 24 hours. A well-maintained knowledge graph counters this by:

Redundancy - Multiple paths to same information
Context - Links provide retrieval cues
Active Use - Writing, connecting, reviewing

Best Practices

Review notes before they become stale
Update outdated information promptly
Create Evergreen Notes that evolve over time

Link-Quality-Rule.title: Link Quality Rule
Link-Quality-Rule.content:
Link Quality Rule
Links should be meaningful and purposeful, not just decorative.
Rule

Only link when there’s a substantive connection
Each link should answer: “Why should the reader follow this link?”
Avoid linking for the sake of linking

Test Criteria (for AI Evaluation)

 Can you explain WHY each link exists in one sentence?
 Does each link add genuine value to the current note?
 Is there a minimum of 2-3 links per note?
 Are links substantive, not just keyword matches?
 Would removing any link lose meaningful context?

Quality Guidelines





















Good LinkBad LinkExplains a concept used in current noteRandom keyword matchProvides deeper context on a related idea”Also see…” without contextConnects to a concrete related conceptLink to everything mentioned
Link Density

Minimum: 2-3 links per note (ensures connectivity)
Maximum: Avoid over-linking (dilutes meaning)
Target: Links that add genuine value

Agent Responsibility
When adding new notes:

Find truly related concepts via graph_related
Only link to notes with meaningful connection
Verify links enhance understanding

Related

Atomic Note Principle
Linking Principle

Linking-Principle.title: Linking Principle
Linking-Principle.content:
Linking Principle
Notes must be interconnected through Wikilinks to form a knowledge graph.
Rule

Every note should link to at least 2-3 related notes
Links should be content-driven — only link when there’s a meaningful connection
Use descriptive link text: [[Concept|Display Text]] when appropriate
Always consider: “What does this note relate to?”

Test Criteria (for AI Evaluation)

 Does each note have 2-3 outgoing links?
 Are links meaningful (pass the “why follow?” test)?
 Can you explain each link’s purpose in one sentence?
 Are links bidirectional where appropriate?

Why It Matters

Links create the graph structure that enables efficient traversal
Connected notes are discoverable through graph tools
The agent can navigate from any point to find related information

Bidirectional Awareness

Most tools automatically show backlinks
You don’t need to manually create backlinks — focus on forward links from atomic notes
Backlinks emerge naturally from good forward linking

Note-Insertion-Strategy.title: Note Insertion Strategy
Note-Insertion-Strategy.content:
Note Insertion Strategy
The agent must be able to find the correct place in the graph to insert new knowledge.
Rule
When adding new information:

Search existing nodes using graph_search to find related concepts
Analyze the graph using graph_get_neighbors and graph_related to understand the context
Identify the most specific node where the new information naturally belongs
Create a new atomic node if no existing node fits the atomic principle
Link appropriately to existing related nodes

Process
1. Receive new information
2. Search: graph_search(keywords)
3. Analyze: graph_get_neighbors_ranked(relevant_node)
4. Decide: Create new? Or add to existing?
5. Link: Connect to 2-3 related nodes
6. Register: Use vault-sources if tracking provenance

Why It Matters

Prevents duplicate nodes
Ensures new knowledge integrates into the existing graph
Makes the graph navigable by both humans and agents

Note-Lifecycle-Management.title: Note Lifecycle Management
Note-Lifecycle-Management.content:
Note Lifecycle Management
Notes have a lifecycle. Knowing when to archive or delete is as important as knowing when to create.
Lifecycle Stages
1. Seedling (New)

Fresh capture from input
Often incomplete, needs processing
Status: needs work

2. Bud (Developing)

Processed, linked, but not fully integrated
Has 2-3+ meaningful connections
Status: growing

3. Evergreen (Mature)

Fully formed, well-linked, frequently referenced
Core knowledge of the vault
Status: stable

4. Archive (Inactive)

Still valid but no longer relevant
May contain historical value
Status: dormant

5. Delete (End of Life)

Outdated, incorrect, or redundant
No retrieval value
Status: removed

When to Archive

No references in 3+ months
Topic no longer active in your life/work
Superseded by updated notes
Belongs to a past project (completed)

When to Delete

Factually incorrect
Duplicate of another note (merge instead)
Purely ephemeral (meeting notes, temporary)
Violates atomicity with no fix

Archive Process

Move to /Archive/ folder
Keep links intact (they become dead ends - acceptable)
Add archived: true frontmatter
Update any MOCs that reference it

Don’t Over-Archive
Most notes should become evergreen. Be conservative:

Useful reference → keep
Potential future value → keep
Only dead weight → archive/delete

Related

Handling Outdated Information
Anti-Patterns in Knowledge Management
Graph Maintenance

Note-Splitting-Principle.title: Note Splitting Principle
Note-Splitting-Principle.content:
Note Splitting Principle
When an atomic note grows too large, it must be split into smaller atomic notes and rewired to maintain graph health.
Rule
Split a note when:

Content exceeds ~200-300 words
Multiple distinct ideas are covered
The note could be broken into separate concepts

Test Criteria (for AI Evaluation)

 Is the note ≤300 words?
 Does the note cover only one idea?
 Are there “also…” or “additionally…” patterns suggesting multiple ideas?
 Can this note be meaningfully linked to 2+ other notes?

Process

Identify distinct ideas in the oversized note
Create new atomic notes for each idea
Preserve core information in the original note
Link to new notes from the original (and vice versa)
Update outgoing links from other notes if needed
Rebuild graph index using graph_build_index

Warning Signs

Note is hard to read at a glance
You find yourself writing “also…” multiple times
The note covers multiple topics

Why It Matters

Keeps the graph traversable
Maintains atomicity
Ensures efficient agent navigation

Example
Original: “Learning Types.md” (too big)
Split into:

“Supervised Learning”
“Unsupervised Learning”
“Reinforcement Learning”
Each links to the others and to the parent concept.

Atomic Note Principle

Note-Taking-Methods-Compared.title: Note-Taking Methods Compared
Note-Taking-Methods-Compared.content:
Note-Taking Methods Compared
Different approaches to capturing and organizing knowledge.
Zettelkasten (Slip Box)
Philosophy: Atomic notes, links, emergence

One idea per note
Dense linking
Emergence through connections
Best for: Research, learning, creative work


PARA (Projects/Areas/Resources/Archives)
Philosophy: Action-oriented organization

Categorize by function, not topic
Focus on outcomes
Nested folders + links
Best for: Productivity, work projects


Cornell Method
Philosophy: Structured note-taking

Cue column (questions)
Notes column (main points)
Summary section
Best for: Classroom notes, studying


Mapping/Mind Mapping
Philosophy: Visual, branching

Central concept
Radiating branches
Visual connections
Best for: Brainstorming, planning


Obsidian/Smart Notes
Philosophy: Combines approaches

Atomic notes (Zettelkasten)
PARA for structure
Bidirectional linking
Best for: Knowledge workers


Which to Choose?





























NeedMethodResearchZettelkastenProjectsPARAStudyingCornellBrainstormingMind MapsPersonal knowledgeObsidian

PARA-Method.title: PARA Method
PARA-Method.content:
PARA Method
PARA is an organizational system for digital life developed by Tiago Forte. It organizes information by function, not topic.
The Four Categories






























CategoryDescriptionExamplesProjectsShort-term outcomesBlog post, app build, event planningAreasOngoing responsibilitiesHealth, finances, careerResourcesTopics of interestRecipes, research, referenceArchivesInactive itemsCompleted projects, old reference
PARA vs Zettelkasten






























AspectPARAZettelkastenFocusAction-orientedKnowledge-orientedStructureHierarchy by domainNetwork by connectionUse casePersonal productivityResearch & learningSynergyWorks well togetherIndependent system
Integration with Second Brain
PARA provides the folder structure while Zettelkasten provides the linking logic:
PARA (where)
├── Projects/
│   └── [Project notes with action items]
├── Areas/
│   └── [Area notes with ongoing focus]
├── Resources/
│   └── [Topic notes - these become Zettelkasten nodes]
└── Archives/
    └── [Completed/inactive]

Within Resources/, use Zettelkasten Method and Linking Principle to build the knowledge graph.

Progressive-Summarization-vs-Zettelkaste.title: Progressive Summarization vs Zettelkasten
Progressive-Summarization-vs-Zettelkaste.content:
Progressive Summarization vs Zettelkasten
Two powerful note-taking methodologies with different philosophies and use cases.
Progressive Summarization
Philosophy: Distill information through multiple passes
Process:

Pass 1: Bold key points in source material
Pass 2: Convert to bullets
Pass 3: Write in your own words
Pass 4: Add links to related notes

Best for:

Books, articles, videos (consumable content)
Passive input processing
Building personal summaries

Creator: Tiago Forte (PARA method)
Zettelkasten
Philosophy: Create atomic, interconnected notes
Process:

Extract one idea per note
Write in your own words
Link to related notes
Let emergence happen

Best for:

Research and writing
Active knowledge creation
Building a lattice of ideas

Creator: Niklas Luhmann
Comparison



































AspectProgressive SummarizationZettelkastenUnitSummaryAtomic noteFocusCompressionConnectionInputConsumable contentAny ideaOutputQuick recallNew insightsTimeFast (15 min/chapter)Slow (1 note/idea)
When to Use Each
Use Progressive Summarization when:

Processing books or articles
Need quick reference summaries
Building a reference library

Use Zettelkasten when:

Doing research or writing
Wanting to generate new ideas
Building a knowledge network

The Best of Both Worlds
Use Progressive Summarization for initial capture, then convert summaries to atomic Zettelkasten notes for linking.

Highlight → Bold → Bullet (Progressive Summarization)
Extract atomic idea → Write in own words (Zettelkasten)
Link to related notes (both)

Related

Progressive Summarization

Progressive-Summarization.title: Progressive Summarization
Progressive-Summarization.content:
Progressive Summarization
Progressive summarization is a note-taking technique developed by Tiago Forte (creator of the PARA method) for efficiently processing large amounts of information.
The Method
Summarize content in multiple passes, each more detailed:






























PassFormatPurpose1Bolded textCapture main points2Bulleted listStructure emerges3Your wordsPersonal interpretation4External linksConnect to network
Why Progressive?

First pass is fast (identify what’s notable)
Later passes deepen understanding only when needed
Balances depth with time efficiency
Creates layered notes for different contexts

Connection to Second Brain
Progressive summarization feeds into Atomic Note Principle:

Each bolded point can become an atomic note
Summary layers provide context for linking
Complements Zettelkasten Method workflow

In Practice

Capture - Save original content (article, transcript, book excerpt)
Distill - Bold key points (pass 1)
Structure - Convert to bullets (pass 2)
Synthesize - Write in your own words (pass 3)
Connect - Link to related notes (pass 4)

Retrieval-Practice.title: Retrieval Practice
Retrieval-Practice.content:
Retrieval Practice
The most effective learning technique: forcing yourself to recall information.
Why Retrieval Works
Retrieval strengthens memory pathways:

Passive re-reading = weak encoding
Active recall = strong memory


“The effort of retrieving learning is where the learning happens.”

How to Practice Retrieval
1. Flashcards

Convert atomic notes to flashcards
Use spaced repetition (see Spaced Repetition)
Focus on connections, not just facts

2. Closed-Book Notes

Try to write from memory
Check against your notes
Fill gaps

3. Teaching

Explain a concept without looking
The Feynman Technique
Identify gaps in understanding

4. Practice Problems

Apply concepts to new scenarios
Test understanding, not just recall

Retrieval in Your Second Brain

Review sessions - Close notes, try to reconstruct
Flashcard creation - From atomic notes
Quiz yourself - Use Dataview to generate questions
Teach others - Share knowledge to verify

Common Mistakes

Passive reading - Just highlighting = no learning
Too soon - Retrieval after 5 minutes < retrieval after 1 day
Too easy - Don’t use multiple choice; use free recall

Related

Spaced Repetition
Knowledge Retention
Writing as Thinking

Search-Strategies.title: Search Strategies
Search-Strategies.content:
Search Strategies in Knowledge Graphs
Finding information efficiently in a graph-based system.
Why Search Different
Unlike folders, you search by connection, not location:





















Folder SearchGraph Search”Where did I put it?""What connects to X?”By date or topicBy relationshipOne pathMultiple paths

Search Techniques
1. Concept Search
graph_search(query)
Find notes by title or content keywords.
2. Neighbor Search
graph_get_neighbors(node)
Find all notes linked from/to a specific note.
3. Related Search
graph_related(node, limit=5)
Find 2-hop connections — notes not directly linked but related.
4. Hub Search
graph_hubs(limit=10)
Find the most connected notes (good starting points).

When to Use Each

























NeedTechniqueKnow the topicConcept SearchExploring a note’s connectionsNeighbor SearchFinding hidden connectionsRelated SearchFinding entry pointsHub Search

Practical Examples
Example 1: Research a New Topic

Start with graph_hubs to find relevant hub
Use graph_get_neighbors to explore
Use graph_related to find unexpected connections

Example 2: Find Where a Note Fits

Create new atomic note
Use graph_search for related keywords
Use graph_get_neighbors_ranked on top results
Add 2-3 most relevant links

Example 3: Discover Gaps

Run graph_isolated_nodes
Find notes with few connections
Link them to relevant topics


Anti-Patterns

Searching by date — Dates don’t matter in graphs
Searching by folder — There are no folders (see Graph vs Hierarchical Filing)
Only using full-text — Relationship search finds better results

Second-Brain-Essence.title: Second Brain Essence
Second-Brain-Essence.content:
Second Brain Essence

The minimal set of rules to bootstrap a healthy, self-improving knowledge base on any topic.

This document contains the distilled essence of how to build a second brain. Use it to initialize any new knowledge vault.

The 3 Core Principles
1. Atomic Notes
Every note contains one single idea. Maximum ~200-300 words.
✅ Good: "# Zettelkasten Method - core principles"
❌ Bad: Combined note about Zettelkasten AND tools AND examples

2. Meaningful Linking
Every note links to 2-3 related notes. Links must be content-driven.
[[Concept]] - brief explanation of how it connects
3. Graph Navigation
Maximum 3 hops to reach any note from a hub. Create hub notes as entry points.

The 5 Essential Rules



































RuleTriggerActionAtomicNote >300 wordsSplit into atomic notesLinkNew note addedLink to 2-3 related notesHubTopic growsCreate hub note as entry pointTraverseFinding infoMax 3 hops from hubHealthRegular checkFix isolated notes

The Maintenance Cycle
Run every 15 minutes (or daily for manual):
1. git pull
2. Check graph: graph_hubs, graph_isolated_nodes
3. Read 3-5 random notes
4. Fix violations (split, link, rewire)
5. git add + commit + push


The Bootstrap Process (Week 1)

































DayAction1Create _root.md (vault purpose)2Add Atomic Note Principle + Linking Principle3Process 1 document → 3-5 atomic notes4Link notes together5Run health check, fix issues6-7Repeat, aim for 10 notes
Target: 10 notes → 25 → 50 → 100

The MCP Tools
Use vault-graph MCP for navigation:

graph_hubs — Find entry points
graph_isolated_nodes — Find orphans
graph_search(query) — Find by keywords
graph_get_neighbors(note) — Get connections


The Anti-Patterns
Avoid:

Note hoarding (capture everything → process nothing)
Over-linking (links without meaning)
Skipping maintenance (only add, never fix)
Perfectionism (never publish because “not ready”)


Quick Start Template
# [Topic] Knowledge Base
 
## Purpose
[What does this vault cover?]
 
## Core Principles
- [[Atomic Note Principle]]
- [[Linking Principle]]
 
## Hub Notes
- [Topic] Hub → main entry point
 
## Daily Practice
- Capture → Process → Link → Review

Why This Works

Atomic → Reusable across contexts
Linked → Discoverable, navigable
Hub-based → 3-hop traversal
Maintained → Self-improving

The compound effect: more notes → more connections → more emergence → more value.
