Second-Brain-Seed.title: Second Brain Seed
Second-Brain-Seed.content:
Second Brain Seed

Drop this file into any new vault’s CLAUDE.md to bootstrap a healthy, self-improving knowledge base.
Every rule below has been tested and proven by a live, AI-maintained knowledge vault.


1. Best Practices Conformance
Rules that ensure any new knowledge base starts healthy.
Rule: Maintain human ownership over AI-assisted knowledge work.
Why: Delegating thinking to AI degrades the cognitive benefits of knowledge management.
Test: Check if you can articulate the key insights from notes you created with AI help without looking at them.
Rule: Process matters more than output — writing is thinking.
Why: The act of writing consolidates understanding; outsourcing it loses the learning.
Test: After AI drafts a note, do you rewrite it in your own words? If not, you’re skipping the thinking.
Rule: Verify all AI suggestions, don’t trust them.
Why: AI can hallucinate facts, links, and relationships that don’t exist.
Test: Check 3 AI-suggested links — can you explain WHY each connection exists in one sentence?
Rule: Start with a _root.md note defining the vault’s purpose.
Why: Without a clear purpose, the knowledge base lacks direction and coherence.
Test: Can you state your vault’s purpose in one sentence? Does every note connect back to this purpose?
Rule: Add 3 foundational notes before capturing content — Atomic Note, Linking, Graph Traversal.
Why: Foundation notes establish conventions early; retrofitting is harder than starting right.
Test: Do new notes reference these foundations? Are they linked in new notes’ frontmatter?
Rule: Process documents into atomic notes before saving — capture ≠ save.
Why: Unprocessed bookmarks and copied text don’t become knowledge; only processed notes are discoverable.
Test: Count your unprocessed bookmarks. If > 10, you’re hoarding not building.
Rule: Know what NOT to capture — skip easily retrievable, ephemeral, duplicate info.
Why: Not everything deserves a note; selective capture keeps the vault valuable.
Test: Do you ask “will I need this in 6 months?” before creating notes?
Rule: Knowledge compounds better than money — synergistic returns, no capital needed, non-rivalrous.
Why: Notes combine to create insights greater than the sum; sharing multiplies value.
Test: Can you point to an insight that emerged from combining two notes?
Rule: Build a knowledge legacy — document clearly, share openly, connect broadly.
Why: Knowledge outlasts the creator; notes help future learners.
Test: Could someone understand your notes without you? Would they find them valuable?
Rule: Diversify your knowledge portfolio — core (30%), domain (40%), adjacent (20%), exploratory (10%).
Why: Diversification creates resilience and cross-domain innovation.
Test: Can you categorize your notes into these proportions? Are you balanced?
Rule: Your second brain shapes your knowledge identity — you become someone who connects ideas naturally.
Why: The vault changes how you think; thinking in networks becomes automatic.
Test: Do you naturally see connections between ideas? Has your thinking style changed?
Rule: Your second brain multiplies, not adds — one note connects to many, each connection = insight potential.
Why: Notes compound in value; multiplication beats addition.
Test: Can you trace how one note connects to multiple insights?
Rule: Create a CLAUDE.md file in vault root with vault purpose, structure, and agent guidelines.
Why: AI agents need explicit instructions to navigate and maintain the vault correctly.
Test: Does your CLAUDE.md exist? Does it define the vault’s purpose and agent behavior?
Rule: Externalize, network, augment — the extended mind shift from brain-only to brain+network.
Why: Biological limits constrain memory; networked tools extend recall, association, and intelligence beyond skull limitations.
Test: Have you shifted from memorizing to organizing? Do you think of your vault as an extension of your mind?
Rule: Build a learning stack with 5 integrated layers: capture, processing, storage, review, output.
Why: Individual tools don’t create systems thinking; integrated layers ensure knowledge flows from input to output.
Test: Do you have all 5 layers? Can knowledge flow from capture through to output?
Rule: Use note templates for consistency — atomic note, MOC, project templates.
Why: Templates ensure all needed fields are captured and speed up note creation.
Test: Do you use templates? Can you create a new note from a template in <1 minute?
Rule: Use distinct note types — atomic, MOC, structure, project, reference, archive.
Why: Different purposes need different formats; mixing types creates confusion.
Test: Can you categorize your notes by type? Do you use the right template for each?
Rule: Choose note-taking method based on purpose — Zettelkasten for research, PARA for projects, Cornell for studying.
Why: Different methods suit different needs; forcing one size fits all reduces effectiveness.
Test: Have you chosen methods aligned with your goals? Do different contexts use different approaches?
Rule: Use Progressive Summarization for capture, then convert to atomic Zettelkasten notes for linking.
Why: PS provides fast compression; Zettelkasten provides connection and emergence; combining them gets both benefits.
Test: Do you use both methods? Can you trace knowledge from capture through to linked atomic notes?
Rule: Treat your vault as a knowledge operating system — it manages resources, runs applications, enables functionality.
Why: The OS metaphor captures how second brain organizes resources (knowledge), runs processes (thinking), enables action (decision-making).
Test: Can you articulate what your knowledge OS does? Does it manage resources, enable thinking, power decisions?
Rule: AI agents should make one atomic change per commit.
Why: Large, scattered commits are hard to review and revert; atomic changes are clear and manageable.
Test: Can you describe each commit in one sentence? Does each commit do exactly one thing?
Rule: Write descriptive commit messages that describe the knowledge added/changed.
Why: Vague messages (“iteration 2”) don’t help trace the vault’s evolution.
Test: Can you understand what changed by reading commit messages? Are they informative?
Rule: Ignore cache files — never commit .mcp-cache/, .obsidian/, *.log.
Why: Generated files bloat the repo and cause merge conflicts.
Rule: Use tags to complement links, not replace them — links are stronger connections.
Why: Tags are loose associations; links create navigable relationships.
Test: Can you reach every tagged note via links? Are tags supplemental?
Rule: Use templates for common note types — ensures consistency and complete information capture.
Why: Templates speed up creation and ensure nothing is missed; they capture all needed fields.
Test: Do you have templates? Can you create a new note from a template in <1 minute?
Rule: Capture quickly, process within 24-48 hours — ideas are fleeting.
Why: Ideas escape if not captured fast; processing later loses context and momentum.
Test: Do you have a quick capture method? Can you capture an idea in <2 minutes?
Rule: Don’t over-tag — 2-5 tags per note is plenty.
Why: Excessive tags create noise and dilute meaning.
Test: Count tags per note. Is each tag meaningful? Remove generic tags.
Test: Check your .gitignore — are cache directories excluded?
Rule: Start simple — write notes and link them before creating elaborate templates or folders.
Why: Complexity before content leads to system abandonment; simple first, refine later.
Test: Can you write a note and link it without consulting any templates? If not, you’re over-organizing.
Rule: Track note metadata (last-reviewed, review-count) in frontmatter.
Why: Review history enables spaced repetition and identifies neglected notes.
Test: Do your notes have last-reviewed dates? Can you find stale notes easily?
Rule: Use Zettelkasten principles — atomic notes, unique IDs, meaningful linking.
Why: The method is proven (Luhmann wrote 70+ books from his Zettelkasten); it turns the vault into a thinking partner.
Test: Are your notes atomic? Do they have unique identifiers? Can the graph generate insights through connections?
Rule: Use active retrieval, not passive re-reading — close your notes and try to recall.
Why: The effort of retrieving strengthens memory; passive reading/highlighting doesn’t create learning.
Test: When reviewing, do you close notes and try to recall? Or just re-read?


2. Best Practices Catalog
The testable list of what “healthy” means.
Rule: Engage actively with notes — think before reading.
Why: Passive reading produces no new knowledge; engagement is where connections form.
Test: Before reading a note, ask: “What questions does this raise? What connects to what I already know?”
Rule: Capture thoughts immediately — don’t trust memory.
Why: Ideas fade; writing them down preserves the insight and enables future retrieval.
Test: When a thought arises while reading, do you create an atomic note right away? Or plan to “remember it later”?
Rule: Write to think, not just to record — use the Feynman Technique.
Why: Writing clarifies ideas and reveals gaps in understanding; passive transcription doesn’t create knowledge.
Test: Can you explain a concept from your notes simply? Does writing reveal insights you’d miss just reading?
Rule: Each thought becomes one atomic note, linked to its origin.
Why: Scattered thoughts in long notes are hard to find; atomic notes are discoverable and linkable.
Test: Check your recent notes — can each be understood in isolation? Does it link back to where it originated?
Rule: Only keep what you process into atomic notes — don’t save everything.
Why: Note hoarding creates noise; unprocessed bookmarks and copied text don’t become knowledge.
Test: Count your unprocessed bookmarks. If > 10, you’re hoarding not building.
Rule: Write notes that are self-contained — future you should understand them without clicking links.
Why: Notes that depend on other sources become meaningless when links break or sources disappear.
Test: Can you understand each note without clicking any links? Can you verify each claim from memory?
Rule: Always cite sources and write notes independently of any single source.
Why: Single-source dependency means notes don’t make sense without the original; you can’t verify claims.
Test: Can you understand your note without clicking any links? Can you verify each claim from memory?
Rule: Write notes in your own words — encoding in your own language strengthens retention.
Why: Copy-pasting doesn’t create understanding; rewriting consolidates knowledge.
Test: Can you explain a note’s concepts without looking at it? Did you write it in your own words?
Rule: Manage note lifecycle — seedling → bud → evergreen → archive.
Why: Notes evolve; understanding lifecycle prevents over-attachment to outdated notes.
Test: Can you categorize notes by lifecycle stage? Do you archive notes that are no longer relevant?
Rule: Follow insertion strategy — search first, analyze neighbors, create atomic, link appropriately.
Why: Prevents duplicates and ensures new knowledge integrates into the existing graph.
Test: Before adding a note, do you search for existing related nodes? Can you explain where it fits?
Rule: Track metrics — node count, edge density, hub distribution, isolated nodes.
Why: Quantitative metrics reveal health; without tracking, problems go unnoticed.
Test: Do you track node count and edge density? Can you identify imbalances?
Rule: Use graph visualization for exploration, not just pretty pictures — find gaps and clusters.
Why: Visualization reveals structure that text hides; use it to find problems, not decorate.
Test: When did you last use graph view to find gaps? Can you identify underserved areas?
Rule: Use hybrid mind — brain for creativity/judgment, second brain for storage/retrieval.
Why: Each excels at different tasks; over-relying on either reduces effectiveness.
Test: Do you balance brain work (thinking) with external work (storage)?
Rule: Use atomic notes as first principles — combine to form reasoning chains and new insights.
Why: Atomic notes are fundamental concepts that can be freely combined; this enables first-principles thinking.
Test: Can you trace a solution back to atomic fundamentals? Do combinations create new insights?
Rule: Follow the knowledge pipeline: input → capture → process → connect → output.
Why: Structured flow ensures knowledge doesn’t get stuck; output creates feedback for more input.
Test: Can you trace knowledge from input through to output? Is there a flow?
Rule: Practice interleaving — mix topics and create cross-domain connections deliberately.
Why: Cross-domain insights emerge from unexpected connections; single-domain silos miss these opportunities.
Test: Do you link notes across different domains? Can you find analogies between fields?
Rule: Treat your vault as a personal knowledge API — consistent input, processing, output.
Why: Like software APIs, reliable systems produce reliable results; consistency builds trust in the system.
Test: Can you predict what insights will emerge? Is your knowledge system reliable?
Rule: Use graph over hierarchy — link by association, not location.
Why: Graphs enable discovery, serendipity, and emergence; hierarchies constrain and duplicate.
Test: Do you organize by links first? Can you find notes by connection, not folder?
Rule: Embrace network effects — value increases with graph density, not note count.
Why: Graphs improve with scale; folders worsen; the more connections, the more insights emerge.
Test: Is your graph density increasing? Do unexpected connections create new insights?
Rule: Take ownership — curate your own inputs, process actively, build your own structure.
Why: Passive learning produces passive knowledge; ownership drives deeper understanding.
Test: Do you curate your own learning? Do you add your own interpretation to notes?
Rule: Turn consumption into creation — process, add insights, share.
Why: Creation deepens learning and builds reputation; passive consumption alone has limited value.
Test: Do you create from what you consume? Can you point to something you created from your notes?
Rule: Invest in the meta-skill — learning how to learn compounds across all domains.
Why: This skill underlies everything else; returns are infinite.
Test: Do you treat knowledge management as a foundational skill? Does it improve everything else?
Rule: Focus on systems, not goals — daily processes over binary outcomes.
Why: Systems are continuous and actionable; goals focus on outcomes and discourage when missed.
Test: Do you have daily/weekly systems? Can you measure process rather than just outcomes?
Rule: Build legacy — document clearly, share openly, connect broadly.
Why: Knowledge outlives us; good notes help others learn and build on your work.
Test: Could someone understand your notes without you? Would they find them useful?
Rule: Knowledge compounding beats money compounding — no capital needed, synergistic returns, non-rivalrous.
Why: Notes connect to create insights greater than sum; sharing multiplies value.
Test: Can you point to an insight that emerged from combining notes? Do connections exceed notes?
Rule: Use MCP graph tools — graph_search, graph_hubs, graph_related, graph_isolated_nodes.
Why: AI navigation requires graph tools; they enable discovery that manual navigation misses.
Test: Can you use graph tools to find connections? Do you use them for navigation?
Rule: Use descriptive commit messages — describe the knowledge added, not “iteration N”.
Why: Vague messages don’t help trace the vault’s evolution.
Test: Can you understand what changed by reading commit messages?
Rule: Build self-referential accuracy — notes must follow their own rules.
Why: A knowledge base about knowledge management should exemplify its own principles.
Test: Do your notes follow the rules they prescribe? Can you verify self-conformance?
Rule: Skip easily retrievable info — don’t note what web search finds in 5 seconds.
Why: Not all info deserves a note; capturing noise dilutes signal.
Test: Can you find this info quickly online? If yes, skip the note.
Rule: Diversify knowledge portfolio — core (30%), domain (40%), adjacent (20%), exploratory (10%).
Why: Diversification creates resilience and enables innovation from cross-pollination.
Test: Is your knowledge diversified? Do you have both depth and breadth?
Rule: Track provenance — know the source of each piece of knowledge.
Why: Provenance enables verification and helps understand knowledge lineage.
Test: Can you trace each note back to its source? Do you know where knowledge originated?
Rule: Handle outdated information — date everything, use deprecation markers, don’t delete.
Why: Notes become outdated; without handling, the vault becomes unreliable.
Test: Do you have frontmatter with dates? Do you deprecate outdated notes instead of deleting?
Rule: Trust but verify — don’t trust notes blindly, check sources and freshness.
Why: Notes may contain outdated info, misremembered facts, or incomplete understanding.
Test: Do you verify notes before using them? Can you point to recent verifications?
Rule: Use atomic notes as first principles — combine freely to rebuild understanding.
Why: Atomic notes are fundamental concepts; links form reasoning chains; combinations create new principles.
Test: Can you trace a complex idea back to atomic fundamentals? Do combinations create new insights?
Rule: Keep notes atomic — one idea per note, 200-300 word max.
Why: Atomic notes are reusable, linkable, and enable efficient graph traversal; large notes become unwieldy and hard to connect.
Test: Can you summarize this note in one sentence? Does it contain exactly one concept? If it’s > 300 words, split it.
Rule: Review notes using spaced repetition — increasing intervals (1→3→7→14→30 days).
Why: The forgetting curve causes decay; periodic review just before forgetting consolidates long-term memory.
Test: Do you have a review schedule? Can you point to notes reviewed at increasing intervals?
Rule: Links provide retrieval cues — more connections = more recall paths.
Why: When you see a related note, it triggers recall; the graph becomes a memory augmentation system.
Test: Do you use links as memory triggers? Can you trace multiple paths to key concepts?
Rule: Use tools to serve knowledge, not the reverse — don’t let tool selection become procrastination.
Why: Obsessing over the perfect app means no knowledge gets captured; the best tool is the one you actually use.
Test: Have you switched note-taking apps in the last 3 months? If yes, how much knowledge was lost in the transition?
Rule: Adapt the second brain principles to your domain — structure is universal, content is specific.
Why: Universal principles (atomic notes, linking, graph traversal) apply to any field; customize hub nodes and templates for your domain.
Test: Have you identified domain-specific hub nodes? Can you adapt templates for your field?
Rule: Do weekly reviews — Monday inbox, Wednesday graph health, Friday knowledge review.
Why: Weekly hits the sweet spot (daily too frequent, monthly too infrequent); maintains vault health.
Test: Do you have a weekly review habit? Can you summarize last week’s work in one sentence?
Rule: Adapt the second brain to any domain — universal principles (atomic, linking, graph) apply everywhere; only content differs.
Why: The methodology is domain-agnostic; structure remains, content adapts.
Test: Have you identified hub nodes for your domain? Do universal principles apply to your specialty?


3. Node Refinement
How to improve existing notes without breaking them.
Rule: Write new notes from your thoughts, don’t just annotate existing ones.
Why: Annotating blends thoughts with source material; new notes create searchable, linkable assets.
Test: Count your original insights this month vs. annotated existing notes. Are you creating, or just commenting?
Rule: Write for your future self, not for perfection — iterate later.
Why: Perfectionism leads to no notes being published; iteration improves notes over time.
Test: Have you revised any note more than 3 times without publishing? Ship it and improve later.
Rule: Review 3 random notes daily — update, link, or confirm they’re fresh.
Why: Regular review catches outdated info, finds linking opportunities, and reinforces knowledge.
Test: Do you have a daily review habit? Can you point to 3 notes reviewed this week?
Rule: Iterate on notes rather than abandoning them — evolve from seedling to evergreen.
Why: Abandoned notes become dead ends; evolved notes compound in value over time.
Test: Can you trace a note’s evolution? Has any note grown from seedling to evergreen?
Rule: Only log significant improvements in iteration logs — not every small change.
Why: Logging every commit creates noise; significant milestones are what matter.
Test: Is your iteration log concise? Does it show meaningful progress, not micro-commits?
Rule: Never delete notes — only deprecate them.
Why: Deleted notes break incoming links; deprecation preserves the graph while marking content as outdated.
Test: Have you ever permanently deleted a note? Did it break links? Deprecate instead.

4. Structure Development
How the knowledge base structure should evolve over time.
Rule: Let structure emerge from links, not from folders.
Why: Hierarchical folders constrain discovery; graph connections reveal unexpected relationships.
Test: Do you spend more time organizing folders than linking notes? Stop — links create structure.
Rule: Use PARA for folders (function), Zettelkasten for links (knowledge) — they complement each other.
Why: PARA provides action orientation; Zettelkasten provides knowledge connection; together they cover both productivity and learning.
Test: Do your folders map to Projects/Areas/Resources/Archives? Do Resources/ contain linked knowledge notes?
Rule: Create cross-domain notes — combine insights from different fields.
Why: Emergence happens at intersections; notes from single domains can’t create novel insights.
Test: Do you have notes that link concepts from different domains? Search for cross-domain connections.
Rule: Use hub nodes as entry points for broad topics — every major theme needs a hub.
Why: Hubs provide navigation anchors; without them, the graph is hard to explore.
Test: Run graph_hubs — are your top hubs actually important topics? Or just frequently linked filler?
Rule: Create hub nodes when a topic has 5+ related notes without a central entry point.
Why: Hubs provide navigation shortcuts and reduce depth to any node; without them, exploration is linear.
Test: Run graph_hubs — are your top hubs covering your main topics? Do any topics have 5+ notes but no hub?
Rule: Search by connection, not location — use graph_search, graph_related, graph_hubs.
Why: Unlike folders, graphs let you find information by relationship, not by where it’s stored.
Test: When looking for something, do you search by connection first? Or by folder/date?
Rule: Review hub nodes monthly — add connections, split if too broad.
Why: Hubs can become bloated over time; regular review keeps them focused and useful.
Rule: Maximum 3 hops from any hub to reach any node.
Why: Beyond 3 hops, relevance drops rapidly; deep chains are hard to traverse and indicate structure problems.
Test: Can you reach any note from a hub in ≤3 clicks? If not, create intermediate hubs.
Test: When did you last review your top 5 hubs? Are any becoming unwieldy?


5. Interconnection Integrity
How to verify that links are correct and valuable.
Rule: Link quality over quantity — every link must have context.
Why: Link inflation creates a false sense of connectivity; meaningless links hurt more than help.
Test: Can you explain WHY each note links to another in one sentence? If not, remove the link.
Rule: Every note should link to 2-3 related notes minimum.
Why: Orphan notes are invisible in the graph; connections enable discovery and emergence.
Test: Run graph_isolated_nodes — are any notes truly unlinked? Connect or archive them.
Rule: Create connections through 5 types: direct relevance, contrast/comparison, analogy, cause/effect, category.
Why: Different connection types create different insights; variety strengthens the network.
Test: Can you categorize your links? Do you have all 5 types represented?
Rule: Run graph health checks weekly — check isolated nodes, link density, node sizes.
Why: Neglected vaults accumulate problems; proactive maintenance catches issues early.
Test: When did you last run graph health checks? Is there a weekly maintenance habit?
Rule: Capture quickly, process later — use an inbox but don’t try to fully process at capture time.
Why: Perfect processing at capture time slows down idea capture; separate capture from processing.
Test: Do you have an inbox? Can you add quick notes without fully elaborating them?
Rule: Every link must answer “Why should the reader follow this?” — no decorative links.
Why: Random keyword matches and “also see” links dilute meaning and waste reader time.
Test: Can you explain in one sentence why each link exists? If not, remove it.
Rule: Create multiple paths to important information — redundancy aids retrieval.
Why: Single points of failure mean information becomes unreachable when links break.
Test: Can you reach key notes through multiple routes? Are important concepts linked from multiple notes?


6. Knowledge Enrichment
How to integrate new knowledge into existing notes.
Rule: Use progressive summarization — summarize content in multiple passes, not all at once.
Why: First pass is fast (bold key points), later passes deepen only when needed; this balances depth with time.
Test: Do you capture first, then progressively distill? Or try to write perfect notes in one go?
Rule: Progressive summarization process: capture → bold notable points → convert to bullets → write in your own words → link to network.
Why: Each pass serves a purpose: capture (preserve), distill (identify), structure (organize), synthesize (encode), connect (network).
Test: Can you trace each piece of knowledge through these stages?

7. Note Size Management
Detecting and splitting oversized notes.
Rule: Split notes that exceed 300 words or contain multiple ideas.
Why: Large notes become unwieldy, hard to link, and lose the benefits of atomicity; splitting keeps each note focused and discoverable.
Test: Is the note > 300 words? Does it contain multiple distinct concepts? If yes to either, split it into atomic notes and rewire links.
Rule: Rebuild graph index after bulk additions or major restructuring.
Why: Stale indexes miss new connections and produce incorrect search results.
Test: Did you rebuild the index after the last bulk import? Does graph_search return expected results?
Rule: When splitting: identify distinct ideas, create new atomic nodes, preserve core in original, link bidirectionally, update all affected links.
Why: Splitting without rewiring breaks the graph; bidirectional links maintain navigability.
Test: After a split, can you reach both notes from multiple paths? Are all incoming links rewired?


8. Knowledge Integration
Combining existing notes with new ideas.
Rule: Share synthesized insights, not raw captures — process before sharing.
Why: Raw inbox items lack context; sharing unprocessed notes confuses others and damages credibility.
Test: Before sharing, can you answer: atomic? linked? sourced? readable?

9. Continuous Exploration
Anti-stagnation rules that keep the system developing.
Rule: Use AI for exploration, but keep humans in the loop for decisions.
Why: AI can scale exploration but lacks judgment about what matters to you.
Test: Review your last 5 AI-generated actions — did a human approve each one before committing?
Rule: Use principles over tools — don’t let tool selection become procrastination.
Why: Obsessing over the perfect app means no knowledge gets captured; the best tool is the one you actually use.
Test: Have you switched note-taking apps in the last 3 months? If yes, how much knowledge was lost in the transition?
Rule: Run regular audits — don’t only add, never review.
Why: Neglected vaults accumulate broken links, outdated info, and dead ends; maintenance keeps health.
Test: When did you last run a health check? If > 30 days ago, schedule an audit.
Rule: Follow growth targets — start small (10 nodes), scale gradually (25→50→100).
Why: Rapid growth without foundation leads to a messy vault; gradual growth builds habits.
Test: Are you hitting weekly node targets? Does the vault feel manageable at current size?
Rule: Configure MCP servers (vault-graph, vault-sources) for AI navigation.
Why: AI agents need graph tools to discover connections and navigate the knowledge base effectively.
Test: Are MCP servers configured? Can AI agents use graph_search and graph_hubs?
Rule: Consistency matters — don’t skip days.
Why: Compound effects depend on regular contribution; sporadic bursts underperform consistent small gains.
Test: How often do you add notes? Is there a regular cadence (daily/weekly)?
Rule: Trust the compound — effects take time to emerge.
Why: Early notes feel isolated, but network effects kick in around 50-100 notes; don’t abandon before the tipping point.
Test: Are you past 50 notes? Do you see organic connections forming?
Rule: Daily review (10 min) beats weekly review (1 hour) — consistency compounds.
Why: Daily engagement maintains freshness and creates more connections than sporadic intense sessions.
Test: Do you review daily? Is there a 10-minute routine?
Rule: Don’t just add — synthesize. Create notes from intersections.
Why: Emergence requires combining existing notes; passive accumulation doesn’t create new insights.
Test: When did you last create a note from combining two existing notes? Can you point to it?
Rule: Run a feedback loop — REFLECT → backlog → execute → metrics → REFLECT.
Why: Without structured iteration, vaults stagnate; the loop ensures continuous improvement.
Test: Is there a regular cycle? Does the vault improve itself over time?
Rule: Refill backlog before it empties — never run out of tasks.
Why: Empty backlog leads to aimless sessions; pre-filled backlog ensures productive heartbeats.
Test: Is the backlog always populated? Are there at least 5 items ready?
Rule: Push through the startup phase — early notes feel useless but momentum builds.
Why: The knowledge flywheel requires initial effort; once spinning, insights emerge with less effort.
Test: Have you passed the tipping point where adding notes triggers multiple connections automatically?
Rule: Use graph_hubs to find entry points, graph_related for discovery.
Why: Hubs reveal the vault’s core concepts; 2-hop exploration reveals unexpected connections.
Test: Can you name your top 5 hubs? Have you used graph_related to find hidden connections?
Rule: Build a curiosity loop: capture → connect → discover → more curiosity.
Why: Each turn amplifies the next; the vault becomes a self-reinforcing learning engine.
Test: Does adding a note make you want to add more? Does the graph reveal unexpected insights?
Rule: Think in decades, not days — don’t optimize for today’s results.
Why: Knowledge compounds over years; short-term stress about structure misses the long-term value.
Test: Can you imagine your vault in 10 years? Are you building for the long game?
Rule: Innovation comes from combining ideas — new+new, existing+existing, or new+existing.
Why: The vault’s value is in making combinations possible; diverse knowledge enables more innovations.
Test: Have you created a note from combining two existing notes? Can you point to it?
Rule: Knowledge compounding beats money compounding — no capital required, synergistic returns (A+B > A+B), non-rivalrous sharing.
Why: Unlike money, knowledge has no startup cost, creates emergence, and multiplies when shared.
Test: Have you experienced knowledge synergy? Has combining notes created insights neither had alone?

Second-Brain-Workflow.title: Second Brain Workflow
Second-Brain-Workflow.content:
Knowledge Management Workflow
A practical end-to-end workflow for building and maintaining a knowledge base. Works for human-only, AI-assisted, or fully autonomous systems.
The Full Pipeline
Input → Capture → Process → Connect → Maintain


1. Input (Ingestion)
Sources:

Books, articles, videos
Conversations and meetings
Ideas and observations
Research and experiments

For AI-assisted systems: Agents can autonomously capture from APIs, web searches, document processing, and more.
Tools:

Readwise, Instapaper (web articles)
Voice memos (voice-to-text)
Kindle highlights
Email clippings


2. Capture (Quick Save)
Save raw material without processing:

Use inbox/note to capture quickly
Add source URL/reference
Tag with broad category
Don’t organize yet — just capture


3. Process (The Key Step)
This is where Progressive Summarization meets Atomic Note Principle:

Review - Read through raw capture
Extract - Bold key points
Distill - Write in your own words
Split - Separate into atomic notes (one idea each)
Source - Add references

Time per note: 5-15 minutes max.

4. Connect (Linking)
After processing, link to existing knowledge:

Search: graph_search(keywords)
Find neighbors: graph_get_neighbors_ranked
Add 2-3 meaningful links per note
Update relevant MOCs if needed

See Note Insertion Strategy.

5. Maintain (Ongoing)
Per Self-Improvement Cycle:

Run graph health checks weekly
Review isolated nodes
Update outdated notes
Split overgrown notes


Daily Practice





















TimeActionMorningProcess 3-5 notes from inboxThroughoutCapture new inputs quicklyWeeklyRun graph maintenance

Tools We Use

Obsidian - Primary note-taking (graph-linked)
vault-graph MCP - Navigate and analyze
vault-sources MCP - Track provenance
AI agents - Can assist with capture, processing, linking, and maintenance

AI-Assisted Enhancements






























TaskHuman OnlyAI-AssistedCaptureManual entryAutonomous ingestionProcessing5-15 min/noteAI can draft, human refinesLinkingManual searchAI suggests connectionsMaintenanceWeekly checksContinuous monitoring

Second-Brain-for-Software-Development.title: Second Brain for Software Development
Second-Brain-for-Software-Development.content:
Second Brain for Software Development
A second brain supercharges your development workflow by capturing patterns, solutions, and insights.
Hub Nodes for Dev
Core Knowledge

Code Patterns - Reusable solutions to common problems
Debugging Tactics - Systematic approaches to fixing bugs
Tool Mastery - IDE, CLI, and environment configs
Architecture Decisions - Trade-offs and reasoning

Integration with Zettelkasten

Each bug fix → atomic note
Each pattern discovered → atomic note
Link solutions to problems via graph_related

Capture Workflow for Devs
1. Read code/article → Extract key insight
2. Write atomic note (~100-200 words)
3. Tag: #pattern, #bug, #tool, #architecture
4. Link to 2-3 related notes
5. Push to vault

Domain-Specific Tools

Dendron - VS Code-native PKM
Obsidian + Shell commands
Git-based snippets with frontmatter

Knowledge Types in Dev

Self-Improvement-Cycle.title: Self-Improvement Cycle
Self-Improvement-Cycle.content:
Self-Improvement Cycle
The second brain is a living system that continuously improves itself through structured iteration. This vault runs a feedback loop that diagnoses, course-corrects, and refills.
Heartbeat Architecture (Current)
The Feedback Loop
REFLECT --> fills backlog --> EXPLORE/AUDIT/CONNECT/DISTILL --> consumes backlog --> metrics update --> REFLECT evaluates --> adjusts priorities

Modes

REFLECT - Diagnose vault health, course-correct problems, refill backlog
EXPLORE - Research and create new atomic notes
AUDIT - Verify existing notes against principles
CONNECT - Strengthen structural gaps in the graph
DISTILL - Extract patterns into Exportable Rules

Decision Logic

Backlog < 5 items → REFLECT
Staleness is high → REFLECT
Consecutive no-commit >= 5 → REFLECT
Otherwise → pop top backlog item

Execution (Every Heartbeat)
Step 1: Pull
git pull origin main
Step 2: Read State

Read Heartbeat State.md for metrics
Read Heartbeat Backlog.md for queue

Step 3: Decide Mode

Apply decision logic above
Pop specific item from backlog if not REFLECT

Step 4: Execute

Run the mode’s specific task
Quality gate: verify before committing

Step 5: Commit & Update

Update metrics in Heartbeat State.md
Mark item complete in Heartbeat Backlog.md
Commit with format: [Mode] What changed
Push to origin

Staleness Detection





















ScoreCriterialowCommitted in last 3 heartbeats, backlog 5+mediumNo commit 3-5 heartbeats OR backlog 2-4highNo commit 5+ heartbeats OR backlog empty OR edit failures > 3
Backlog Format
- [ ] [MODE] Specific actionable task
- [x] [MODE] Completed task (date)
Why This Works

Feedback loop - Each iteration improves the next
Prioritization - REFLECT ensures never stuck on low-value tasks
Metrics-driven - Staleness detection catches stagnation
Self-referential - This vault demonstrates its own methodology

Related

Sources-Provenance-MCP.title: Sources Provenance MCP
Sources-Provenance-MCP.content:
Sources Provenance MCP

GitHub: github.com/pascalweiss/vault-sources-mcp

The vault-sources MCP server tracks the provenance of knowledge — which input sources (transcripts, book chapters, articles) generated which notes in the vault.
Purpose
When an AI agent processes information and creates notes, it’s useful to know:

Where did this information come from?
Which source inspired this note?
What’s the lineage of this knowledge?

Available Tools
db_status
Check the database status.
db_status()

db_init
Initialize the provenance database.
db_init()

generate_note_id
Generate a unique ID for a note.
generate_note_id()

register_note
Register a note with its source.
register_note(note_id: "note-id", source_id: "source-id")

mark_note_deleted
Mark a note as deleted.
mark_note_deleted(note_id: "note-id")

How It Works
The vault-sources MCP maintains a separate SQLite database (.vault-sources.sqlite) that tracks:

Input sources (documents, conversations, URLs)
Generated notes
Links between sources and notes

This enables provenance tracking — knowing the origin of every piece of knowledge in your vault.

Spaced-Repetition.title: Spaced Repetition
Spaced-Repetition.content:
Spaced Repetition
Spaced repetition is a learning technique that schedules review at increasing intervals to optimize long-term memory retention.
The Science
Based on the forgetting curve (Ebbinghaus, 1885): information decays exponentially without reinforcement. Spaced repetition counters this by reviewing material just before forgetting.
Intervals





























ReviewInterval1st1 day2nd3 days3rd7 days4th14 days5th30 days
Application to Note-Taking
In a second brain context:

Active recall from notes strengthens memory
Notes should be reviewed periodically (see Self-Improvement Cycle)
Connect to Zettelkasten Method for atomic note review
Complements Self-Improvement Cycle for vault maintenance

Tools
Popular spaced repetition systems:

Anki (flashcard software)
SuperMemo (SM-2 algorithm)
RemNote (notes + SR combined)

In Your Vault

Tag notes for periodic review
Create flashcards from atomic notes
Link related notes to reinforce context

The-Art-of-Connecting-Notes.title: The Art of Connecting Notes
The-Art-of-Connecting-Notes.content:
The Art of Connecting Notes
Making meaningful connections between ideas.
Why Connections Matter
Connections create understanding:

Single notes = facts
Connected notes = knowledge
Dense graph = insight

What Makes a Good Connection?
1. Direct Relevance
Notes that explain or build on each other:
[[Moving Averages]] → [[MACD]] (MACD uses MA)

2. Contrast/Comparison
Notes that illuminate by opposition:
[[Bull Market]] → [[Bear Market]]

3. Analogy
Notes from different domains with similar patterns:
[[Ecosystems]] → [[Business Ecosystems]]

4. Cause/Effect
Notes where one explains the other:
[[Volatility]] → [[Risk Management]]

5. Category
Notes that share a common parent:
[[RSI]] → [[Momentum Indicators]]

Anti-Patterns
1. False Connections
Linking because you “should” not because it makes sense.
2. Generic Links
“See also” without context.
3. Over-Connecting
Every note → every note.
How to Find Connections

Review neighbors - What does this note connect to?
Use graph_related - Find 2-hop connections
Write and ask - “What does this remind me of?”
Cross-pollinate - Different domains connect unexpectedly

The Connection Test
Can you explain WHY these notes connect in one sentence?

The-Art-of-Not-Taking-Notes.title: The Art of Not Taking Notes
The-Art-of-Not-Taking-Notes.content:
The Art of Not Taking Notes
Not every piece of information deserves a note. Knowing what to skip is as important as knowing what to capture.
When NOT to Take Notes
1. Easily Retrievable Information

Phone numbers, addresses, quick facts
Anything a web search can find in 5 seconds
Documentation that won’t disappear

2. Ephemeral Conversations

Meeting notes that won’t be referenced again
Quick status updates
One-off questions answered once

3. Other People’s Private Info

Don’t become a data hoarder’s assistant
Information not actionable for you

4. Duplicate Knowledge

If you already have a note covering this, update it instead
Check with graph_search before creating

5. Momentum Capture

Don’t pause a deep conversation to take notes
Capture the essence later, stay in the flow

The Reverse Test
Before creating a note, ask:

Will I need this in 6 months?
Does this connect to something I already know?
Is this my insight or someone else’s?

If no → skip. Your second brain will thank you.

The-Forgetting-Curve.title: The Forgetting Curve
The-Forgetting-Curve.content:
The Forgetting Curve and Its Implications
Understanding memory decay to optimize your second brain.
The Science (Ebbinghaus, 1885)
Hermann Ebbinghaus discovered that information decays rapidly:

25% remembered after 1 day
33% remembered after 2 days
28% remembered after 6 days
25% remembered after 31 days

This is the forgetting curve.
The Implications for Knowledge Management
1. Review Timing
Review notes BEFORE forgetting:

First review: 1 day after creating
Second review: 3 days later
Third review: 7 days later
Fourth review: 14 days later
Fifth review: 30 days later

This is the basis of Spaced Repetition.
2. Active Engagement
Passive re-reading does little. Active Retrieval Practice is key:

Close your notes and try to recall
Test yourself with flashcards
Apply concepts to new situations

3. Meaningful Connections
Links in your graph provide retrieval cues:

When you see a related note, it triggers recall
More connections = more retrieval paths
This is why Linking Principle matters

4. Regular Maintenance
Per Self-Improvement Cycle and Graph Maintenance:

Review notes before they go stale
Update outdated information
Strengthen weak connections

Counteracting the Curve

























FactorEffectMeaningful encodingSlower decaySpaced reviewExtends retentionActive retrievalStrengthens memoryMultiple contextsRicher encoding
Second Brain Application

Create notes with strong connections (more retrieval cues)
Review systematically using spaced repetition
Update regularly to reinforce memory
Link extensively to create retrieval network

See Knowledge Retention for more.

Trust-and-Verify.title: Trust and Verify

Trust and Verify in Knowledge Management
Don’t trust your notes blindly - verify and update.
The Problem
Your notes may contain:

Outdated information
Misremembered facts
Incomplete understanding
Source rot

Trust, But Verify
1. Source Verification
Always cite sources. When you can’t verify, mark note:
---
verified: false
---
2. Freshness Check
Review notes periodically:

Tech notes: 6 months
Concepts: 1 year
History: 2 years

3. Contradiction Detection
When you find contradictions:

Don’t delete either note
Add context explaining both
Note which is more current

4. Update, Don’t Replace
Per Evergreen Notes:

Keep history of changes
Note what changed and why
Link to deprecated versions

Red Flags

























FlagActionOld created dateVerify accuracyNo sourcesAdd or mark unverifiedContradictionAdd contextBroken linksFix or update
The Verification Habit

When using a note, glance at frontmatter
If old, schedule verification
When verifying, check sources
Update frontmatter modified date

Process each item into atomic notes
Add frontmatter (tags, source)
Link to related notes

Wednesday: Graph Health (10 min)

Run graph_isolated_nodes - fix orphans
Run graph_hubs - check distribution
Look for broken links
Note any issues

Friday: Knowledge Review (10 min)

Review 5 random notes
Add new connections if possible
Update any stale notes
Write what you learned this week

What to Track
Week: 2026-W10
Notes added: 5
Links added: 12
Issues fixed: 2
Insights: [Brief notes]

Adjust to Your Pace

New users: Start with 15 min/week
Growing vault: 30 min/week
Mature vault: 20 min/week maintenance

Zettelkasten-Method.title: Zettelkasten Method
Zettelkasten-Method.content:
Zettelkasten Method
The Zettelkasten (German for “slip box”) method is the foundation of our second brain system.
Origin
Developed by Niklas Luhmann, a German sociologist who wrote 70+ books and 400+ academic articles using only his Zettelkasten.
Core Philosophy

Atomic notes — One idea per note
Unique IDs — Each note has a unique identifier for direct addressing
Linking — Notes connect to related notes, forming a web of knowledge

How It Works
graph LR
    A[Idea 1] --> B[Atomic Note 1]
    C[Idea 2] --> D[Atomic Note 2]
    B --> D[Links to]
    D --> B[Backlinks]
    B --> E[Atomic Note 3]
    E --> D

The Luhmann Principle

“I do not think for myself, I let my Zettelkasten think for me.”

The system becomes a thinking partner that generates insights through connections.
Key Differences from Traditional Notes

























TraditionalZettelkastenHierarchical foldersNetwork of linksNotes stand aloneNotes are interconnectedHard to find connectionsGraph reveals connectionsNotes get lostEvery note is discoverable
Our Implementation
We use Atomic Note Principle and Linking Principle to implement Zettelkasten digitally.

Zettelkasten-Origins.title: Zettelkasten Origins
Zettelkasten-Origins.content:
Zettelkasten Origins
The historical foundation of the linked note-taking method.
Luhmann’s Method
Niklas Luhmann developed the Zettelkasten method:

German term: “slip box”
Used as his primary thinking tool
Produced 70+ books and 400+ articles

The Key Insight
Luhmann had two filing systems:

Bibliographic notes - References
Permanent notes - His own thoughts

The magic: Permanent notes were written in his own words, in sequence, with links.
The Numbering Myth
Common misconception: Zettelkasten uses sequential IDs (1, 1a, 1b, 1b1…)
Reality: Luhmann used links, not IDs. The ID system was a later interpretation.
The Essential Rules (Luhmann)

Write one idea per note
Write in your own words
Link notes together
Add permanent notes sequentially
Never delete - only deprecate

Modern Adaptation
We use wikilinks instead of IDs:
[[Concept]] instead of "Note 1a"

This is actually closer to Luhmann’s original method than the ID system.

_root.title: _root
_root.content:
Second Brain Vault

“Knowledge is only as valuable as the connections it enables.”

Purpose
This vault is designed to explore and implement a Second Brain system powered by an AI agent that autonomously:

Collects data from various sources (research, conversations, readings, observations)
Processes and structures that data into atomic knowledge nodes
Adds knowledge to this vault following strict Zettelkasten principles
Builds a growing graph of interconnected ideas over time

Core Principles
1. Atomic Nodes
Every note (node) in this vault represents one single idea. This maximizes reusability and allows connections across different contexts.
2. Interconnected Graph
Nodes are linked to other nodes through Wikilinks. The goal is to create an increasingly dense network of knowledge where:

Each node links to related concepts for deeper understanding
Bidirectional connections (backlinks) emerge naturally
The graph grows organically with both nodes and edges

3. Continuous Growth
The vault should grow over time through:

Autonomous agent收集 (collection)
Systematic processing into atomic units
Active linking and cross-referencing

The Vision
We are building a living knowledge system where:

New information is always processed into atomic notes
Every note has a clear purpose and connects to other relevant notes
The graph structure emerges from the content itself
The agent actively contributes to expanding and connecting knowledge

Getting Started

Use the navigate-vault skill to explore the graph
Use the connect-notes skill when adding new notes
Use the rename-note and delete-note skills for maintenance
All notes should follow the atomic principle: one idea per note

Rules & Guidelines
The complete set of rules for maintaining this second brain:

Second Brain Rules — Consolidated rules (start here)
Atomic Note Principle — One idea per node
Linking Principle — Connect with meaningful links
Note Insertion Strategy — Where to add new knowledge
Note Splitting Principle — When and how to split nodes
Link Quality Rule — Meaningful links only
Graph Traversal Efficiency — Maximum 3 hops navigation
Graph Maintenance — Regular health checks
Self-Improvement Cycle — 15-minute iteration loop
HEARTBEAT.md — Autonomous heartbeat cycle for self-improvement
Exportable Rules — Distilled rules for bootstrapping new vaults
Domain-Specific Knowledge Bases — Adapting for different fields


This vault is a collaboration between human and AI, building a second brain that thinks alongside us.
